segments.s226-g225: ز دریا is a fronted prepositional phrase 'from the sea'. نهنگی (nahanag-i) is the subject with the adjectival/indefinite suffix -ی, 'a whale' or 'sea-monster'. بجنگ آمدست is an older/perhaps poetic periphrasis: بجنگ (be-jang, 'to war/into battle') + آمدست (آمد است, 'has come'), i.e. 'has come to fight'. Word order is verb-final/older Persian: prepositional phrase → subject → verbal phrase.
segments.s226-g226: که introduces a relative clause 'whose/which'. جوشنش is جوشن + ـش (jūshan + -ash) = 'his/its cuirass/armor'. چرم پلنگ literally 'leather of (a) leopard' is a noun phrase describing the armor; آمدست here functions as a perfect/copular-like verb 'has come/appeared' used idiomatically to mean 'is (made of)'. Together: 'whose armor is leopard leather.'
segments.s226-g227: بیامد is a simple past 'he came'. بخاقان should be read as به خاقان 'to the Khaghan/king' (prepositional phrase marking the addressee), but the provided lemma/senses do not match this reading. چنین 'thus/so' modifies گفت 'said'; باز here means 'again' (adverb). The sequence is: he came to the Khaghan, and again said thus.
segments.s226-g228: که introduces the content of what was said ('that/that which'). این رزم کوتاه ما is a noun phrase 'this short battle of ours' (رزم 'battle' followed by the adjective کوتاه 'short' and the possessive/agent ما 'our'). شد دراز is a verb + predicate adjective construction: شد 'became' + دراز 'long' = 'has become long.'
segments.s226-g229: از introduces a partitive/source 'from/of'. این نامداران 'these renowned ones' (نامداران = famous men) and هر کشوری 'of every country/realm' — the phrase expresses 'from these famed men of each realm' (هر distributes over کشوری). Word order places the genitive/qualifier after the head in a compact, somewhat poetic sequence.
segments.s226-g230: ز هر سو 'from every quarter/direction' and که introduces a relative clause 'which/where'. بد نامور is a compound meaning 'ill‑famed / badly renowned' (بد modifying نامور), and مهتری is an old noun meaning a chief/one of preeminence; syntactically this clause points to 'from every side from which an ill‑famed chief...' (the verb completing the thought comes in the following line).
segments.s226-g231: بیاورد is a 3rd-person perfective/formal verb meaning “he brought” (classical Persian uses بیاورد where modern Persian might use آورد). و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” این رنجها شد به باد is an idiomatic clause: شد (became) + به باد (literally “to the wind”) = “was blown away / came to nothing / was wasted.” The noun رنجها (plural) is the grammatical subject of شد (the sufferings were lost).
segments.s226-g232: کجا is the interrogative “where.” خیزد is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of خاستن used poetically to mean “arise” or “spring up.” از introduces the source “from / on account of,” modifying کار بیداد (a compound idea: کار = deed/affair; بیداد = injustice/oppression). داد here functions as a noun in the sense of a public outcry/appeal/idea of justice tied to the preceding words (poetically placed after بیداد); the clause asks rhetorically where any outcry/response could arise from such an oppressive deed.
segments.s226-g217: همی is a continuous/iterative particle here ('still/kept on'); از پی دوده is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the wake/after the smoke' (از + پی + noun). هر کس is the indefinite subject phrase 'every/each person' and بدرد is used predicatively/intransitively here ('in pain/afflicted'), but note the main verb of the clause (ببارید, 'fell/rained') comes in the next segment — so this segment establishes who is affected.
segments.s226-g218: ببارید is the past/past-impersonal sense of باریدن ('it rained / fell down'); بر introduces the target ('upon'). ارغوان is a colour/cloth reference ('crimson/purple') serving as the surface, and آب زرد ('yellow water') is the thing that falls. The verb governs the preceding subject phrase (هر کس بدرد) so the whole means the yellow water rained/fell upon each afflicted person/onto the crimson.
segments.s226-g219: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past tense verb 'heard' placed before its subject (poetic inversion); پیران is the subject 'the elders'. دلش ('his heart') is the logical affected noun and خیره گشت is a compound predicate 'became astonished/stunned' (گشت = 'became/turned'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb before subject).
segments.s226-g220: ز here means 'from/at the sound of' (poetic use). آواز ایشان = 'their voice/their call' is the cause phrase, and رخش تیره گشت is another result clause: رخش 'his steed' + تیره گشت 'became dark/turned dark'. The two clauses are parallel: hearing caused inner astonishment and caused the steed's color/mood to darken.
segments.s226-g221: بدل here is an adverbial/phrasing meaning 'in return' or 'in reply'; گفت is the past verb 'he said' (poetic noun senses in the lemma list aside, here it functions as the verb). کای is the vocative particle 'O', and زار و بیچارگان are the addressed epithets 'wretched and hapless ones'. The phrase is a direct address introduced by بدل گفت.
segments.s226-g222: پر از is the fixed combination 'full of' (پر = 'full', از = 'of'); درد و تیمار lists the sorrows 'pain and care/treatment' and غمخوارگان is a plural noun 'those who share/comfort in grief' — here it completes the vocative description of the addressees. The sequence is a string of nominal qualities describing the people addressed.
segments.s226-g223: ندارید is the present/past-negative form 'you do not have' (here 'you lack'); ازین is the compound 'of this' (از + این written archaically), and اگهی (in context) functions as 'knowledge/awareness' though the provided lemma senses are limited — the phrase بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'. Together the segment says 'you undoubtedly have no knowledge of this.'
segments.s226-g224: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'; ایدر means 'here'; شما را uses را as an emphasis/definite marker with 'you (pl.)'; سرآمد زمان is a noun phrase 'the conclusion/end of time' (سرآمد = 'conclusion/end' + زمان = 'time'). The clause states what they lack knowledge of: that here is their end of time.
segments.s226-g177: ببینی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive or narrative formula meaning 'you will see' or 'you see'. که is the conjunction 'that' introducing the subordinate clause. نه ... نه ... is the correlative negator 'neither ... nor ...'; شاه (subject) precedes the verb and ماند (past/ stative verb) follows, so the clause literally 'no king remained'.
segments.s226-g178: This continues the same negative series with another neither...nor phrase. پیلان جنگی is a noun (پیلان, elephants) qualified by جنگی 'war-/military' (postnominal adjective), and این تخت عاج is 'this throne (تخت) of ivory (عاج)' where عاج names the material.
segments.s226-g179: بدین (= بد + این) functions adverbially as 'thus/by this' linking from the previous clause; the word list splits it, so دین is not used independently here. شاددل is an epithet 'glad‑hearted' placed before شاه 'king' (epithet + noun order). شاه ایران بود is a predicate with the past copula بود 'was'.
segments.s226-g180: غم و درد 'grief and pain' is a coordinated subject phrase; بهر is a preposition meaning 'for/to' governing دلیران 'the brave'. بود is the past copula, so the clause reads literally 'grief and pain were for the brave.'
segments.s226-g181: دریغ here is an interjection 'alas/pity' expressing regret; آن دلیران و چندین سپاه is the noun phrase 'those brave ones and many armies/hosts' that it comments on. Word order is demonstrative/epithet + noun, then coordinated second noun.
segments.s226-g182: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that ...'. با 'with' governs فر 'glory' (the royal radiance). برزند is an archaic 3rd‑person plural verb form (here meaning 'come/arrive' or 'fall/appear' in older usage) and joins with the following coordinate phrase با تاج و گاه ('with crown and occasion/place'), so the clause describes how they advanced/appeared accompanied by glory and crown/station.
segments.s226-g183: بتاراج is a prefixed form meaning 'plundered/laid waste' (be‑ + تاراج). بینی here is the same 2nd‑person 'you see'; together they form 'you will see (them) plundered'. همه 'all' is the object and زین 'of saddles' indicates what is taken; سپس 'afterwards/thereafter' gives the temporal frame.
segments.s226-g184: نه introduces negation: 'not / no'; برگردد is 'to return' (3rd person), and از رزمگه 'from the battlefield' is the source phrase. شاد کس literally 'a happy person' but with the preceding نه it yields 'no one returns from the battlefield happy.'
segments.s226-g233: سر شاه کشور is a possessive/construct: سر (literally “head”) stands before its possessor شاه کشور (“the king of the country”) and here functions metonymically for the ruler/leadership. چنین گشته شد is a verbal phrase in which گشته (perfect participle / verbal adjective) plus شد (past auxiliary) gives a passive/result meaning: “thus came to be / was made so.”
segments.s226-g234: This clause has subject first (سیاوش) followed by an agent-expression (بر دست او ‘by his hand’) and a passive predicate (کشته شد ‘was killed’). بر دست + pronoun expresses agency/means (‘by his hand’); کشته شد is the passive past of کشتن (‘to kill’).
segments.s226-g235: بفرمان is a prepositional phrase meaning “by the order of.” گرسیوز is the name (Garsivaz). کم خرد is an adjective phrase where کم (‘little/insufficient’) modifies خرد (‘wisdom’), i.e. ‘of little wisdom’ = ‘foolish’ — a common fixed epithet.
segments.s226-g236: سر اژدها را uses را as the definite direct-object marker attaching to the noun phrase ‘the dragon’s head.’ کسی نسپرد contains the negative past form نسپرد (ن + سپرد ‘did not hand/entrust’); in context کسی takes the sense ‘no one’ because it occurs with the negative verb, yielding ‘no one handed it over.’
segments.s226-g237: This is a simple nominal clause with the copula بود (past ‘was’). جهاندار and پرمایه are coordinated predicates (joined by و): جهاندار ‘world-ruler/sovereign’ and پرمایه ‘richly endowed/substantial.’ بود places the description in the past.
segments.s226-g238: ورا is a dative/benefactive pronoun meaning ‘to him’ (i.e., referring back to Siawash). رستم زابلی is a name plus nisba (‘Rostam of Zabul’). دایه بود is the predicate ‘was (his) nurse’ with بود as past copula.
segments.s226-g239: هر آنگه که is a temporal/conditional compound meaning ‘whenever’ or ‘each time that.’ او is the subject ‘he.’ جنگ و کین are coordinated direct objects (‘war and vengeance’). آورد is a past-tense verb here meaning ‘brought’ or more causatively ‘caused’/‘wrought.’
segments.s226-g240: همی is an older/poetic habitual or continuous marker (often translated ‘would’ or ‘continually’). آسمان بر زمین آورد is a transitive clause ‘brought the sky upon/against the earth’ — a hyperbolic image; بر here marks direction/overlapping (‘upon’). آورد again is the past narrative verb ‘brought.’
segments.s226-g401: بسی پند و اندرز بشنید و گفت is a nominal phrase + past verb sequence: بسی (an adverb modifying quantity) + two synonyms پند و اندرز 'advice and counsel', followed by the past tense verb بشنید 'he heard'. The conjunction و links بشنید to گفت 'and said' (past), so the whole segment means 'he heard much advice and said...'.
segments.s226-g402: کزین پس نباشد مرا جنگ جفت is a subordinate clause introduced by کزین 'from this / hence', پس 'after/then' giving a temporal/habitual sense. نباشد is a negative present/subjunctive copular form meaning 'there will not be' or 'may there not be'; مرا (here segmented as م + را) expresses possession/experiencer 'for me' (i.e. 'there shall be for me no ...'). جنگ جفت = 'paired/paired battle' (جنگ 'fight, battle' + جفت 'pair'). The clause therefore states 'from now on there will be no paired battle for me.'
segments.s226-g403: شوم گفت بپسیچم این کار تفت begins with شوم, a hortative/wish form 'may I become / may I be (so)' expressing an oath or curse. گفت links this exclamation to what follows. بپسیچم is an irregular 1st‑person prefixed verb form (b‑ + root) — here a colloquial/poetic verb meaning roughly 'I will do/perform/consume' (uncertain lexically), and it governs the following object این کار 'this deed'. تفت (a noun/verb 'scorch/roast') here is used as a verbal noun or infinitival notion attached to کار: 'I will roast/scorch this deed' (i.e. punish or put it to the test).
segments.s226-g404: بخویشان بگویم که ما را چه رفت is parsed as به خویشان (here written بخویشان) 'to the kinsmen/relatives' + بگویم 'I will tell' (subjunctive/intentional 'let me tell' or 'I will tell'), followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که 'that' explaining the content: ما را چه رفت 'what befell us' (ما را with object-marker را, چه 'what', رفت 'went/happened'). So the whole means 'I will tell the kinsmen what has happened to us.'
segments.s226-g405: مرا تخت و گنجست و هم چارپای uses an existential/possessive construction where م(را) functions like 'for me / I have' (here segmented as م + را) and گنجست 'is treasure' acts as the copular assertion: 'there is for me throne and treasure'. و هم چارپای adds 'and also quadrupeds' (چارپای = beasts/animals). Word order places the possessor-expression before the items existentially asserted.
segments.s226-g406: بدیشان نمایم سزاوار جای consists of بدیشان 'to/for them' (به + ایشان) as indirect object, نمایم 'I will show/present' in the 1st person subjunctive/future sense, followed by سزاوار جای 'worthy/appropriate of place' (سزاوار 'fit/appropriate' modifying جای 'place'). The clause means 'I will show/present them to those (people), appropriate to their place.'
segments.s226-g407: چو گفت این بگفتیم کاری رواست opens with چو 'when', followed by گفت این 'he said this'. بگفتیم 'we said' is a past tense reporting verb. کاری here means 'a thing / an action' (object of commentary) and رواست is a copular judgment 'it is fitting/permissible' (روا 'fit/permissible' + ست 'is'). Altogether: 'When he said this, we said that the action is fitting.'
segments.s226-g408: بتوران ترا تخت و گنج و نواست is a locative and possessive clause: بتوران 'in/into Turan', ترا is the 2nd‑person suffix ('for you'), and the list تخت و گنج و نوا 'throne and treasure and song/melody' are predicates; the final است copula is incorporated in نواست 'nava‑ast' meaning 'there is/it is melody' so the phrase states that 'in Turan for you there is a throne, treasure, and music.' Word order is locative → benefactive → items → copula.
segments.s226-g25: بدو is the prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here reported speech) and پیران is the subject 'the elders'. که‌ای is an archaic/exclamatory vocative particle (که + ای) used to address someone — here introducing the name پیلتن (Piltan) as direct address.
segments.s226-g26: درودت is a nominal phrase 'your greeting/blessing' (here used as the thing being wished); the preposition ز 'from' marks origin of the blessing (یزدان = God), followed by و 'and' and another source marker از + انجمن 'from the assembly'. The copular/optative verb (e.g. 'may it be') is understood/elliptical here and supplied by the following clauses.
segments.s226-g27: The clause begins with the ablative/poetic marker ز 'from' + نیکی 'goodness' modifying دهش 'a giving/gift'; آفرین here is the verbal stem 'create/grant' (not the interjection), and the optative particle باد 'may' closes the wish. Word order is noun-modifier (ز نیکی دهش) then verb (آفرین) and finally the target phrase بر تو (upon you) before the optative باد.
segments.s226-g28: فلک را 'the heavens/fate' is marked by را (definite topical object) and گذر 'the passing/course' is the noun; بر نگین تو 'over/onto your gem' gives the target, and the final باد is the same optative 'may' making this another blessing. Here فلک is best read as 'fate/destiny' (poetic usage) and the construction parallels the previous optative clause.
segments.s226-g29: This is two coordinated pieces: ز یزدان سپاس is an ablative-phrase 'thanks from God' (i.e. 'thanks be to God'); و links to بدویم پناه where بدویم is the 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive/hortative 'we hasten/seek' and پناه 'refuge' is its object. The hortative/indicative nuance is poetic — 'we take refuge (in Him)'.
segments.s226-g30: که introduces a content clause 'that/for' and دیدم is the past‑tense first‑person 'I saw'. The enclitic ت is the 2nd‑person suffix marking the direct object 'you' (ترا is expressed as ت + object marker را), زنده 'alive' modifies the state, and بر جایگاه 'upon the seat/place' indicates location/status — a straightforward past report of seeing someone alive on their throne/place.
segments.s226-g31: This is a nominative list of proper names and an epithet: زواره and فرامرز are names, و joins them to زال, and سوار is an appositive noun 'horseman/rider' describing زال. Poetic word order places the epithet after the name.
segments.s226-g32: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that' and او is the pronoun 'he'; ماند is the past/present-stative 'remains/is left' and از خسروان یادگار is a genitive phrase 'a memento/remnant of the kings' (از marking origin). The clause states that he endures as a memorial of past kings.
segments.s226-g537: سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward' and governs the following location phrase. میمنه ('the right [flank/side]') names the direction; it follows the preposition as usual. سی هزار دگر is a quantified noun phrase: سی هزار = 'thirty thousand' and دگر postmodifies it as 'another/more' (i.e. 'thirty thousand more').
segments.s226-g538: کمان is the direct object 'bows'. برگرفتند is a past-tense, third-person plural verb 'they took up/they lifted' (بر- prefix + نوشتند pattern; here بر- signals taking up). The coordinating و links the previous object to a second object phrase: چینی سپر = 'Chinese shield(s)', with چینی functioning as an adjective before سپر.
segments.s226-g539: بقلب اندرون is a prepositional/adverbial cluster meaning 'into the heart / in the innermost interior' (بقلب = 'into the heart', اندرون reinforcing 'inside'). پیل و خاقان چین are coordinated nouns linked to that locative: 'the elephant and the Chinese khaghan' — syntactically these nouns are what the prepositional phrase is describing or qualifying (the passage places these names/figures 'in the inner heart').
segments.s226-g540: همی is an archaic/epic adverbial particle that often gives iterative or continuous colouring to the verb (here rendered 'they did/they were'). برنوشتند is past, third-person plural of نوشتن with the prefix بر- ('they inscribed/wrote upon'). روی زمین is the locative complement 'on the ground' (روی = 'upon/on', followed by زمین = 'ground').
segments.s226-g541: جهان is the subject 'the world'. سربسر is an adverb meaning 'throughout' or 'entirely' modifying the following adjective. آهنین گشته بود is a verbal phrase in past perfect sense: گشته (past participle 'become') with بود as auxiliary = 'had become / was become', so 'had become iron/steely.'
segments.s226-g542: بهر here functions adverbially as 'for/for each'; جایگه‌بر is a compound meaning roughly 'for each place/station' or 'in place (for each post)'. تلی 'a heap/pile' and کشته here is a noun/adjectival use 'the slain' (i.e. 'a heap of the slain'). بود is the past-tense copula 'there was' — overall: 'for every post there was a heap of the slain.'
segments.s226-g543: ز بس is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from so much / because of so great.' ناله نای is a genitive-like sequence 'the moaning of the ney (reed‑flute)' (ناله = 'moan/lament', نای = 'ney/reed flute'). و connects a second noun phrase بانگ درای where بانگ = 'cry/sound' and درای (derived from دارا) qualifies it as 'of the wealthy/possessing' — i.e. 'the cry of the rich' (درای functions as an adjectival/possessive qualifier).
segments.s226-g544: زمین و زمان is a coordinated subject 'earth and time'. اندر آمد is a verbal phrase where اندر (in/into) combines with آمد 'came' to give a sense of being displaced or coming forth; ز جای is an ablative phrase 'from (their) place(s)'. Word order places the subject first, then the verbal phrase with the source expression at the end: 'earth and time came from their places.'
segments.s226-g489: This is a past-tense clause with verb before noun for poetic effect: بترسید (past 'became afraid') + ماه (the moon) is the subject. از پی گفت و گوی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'because of/after the ensuing talk' (از پی = 'in the wake of, because of' + گفت و گوی = 'conversation' where گفت و گوی = گفت‌وگو). The conjunctional و simply links گفت and گوی as the fixed compound 'گفت‌وگو' in poetic spacing.
segments.s226-g490: بخم is an archaic/poetic noun meaning a kind of gloom or obscurity; اندر امد is a two-word verb phrase 'came in/entered' (اندر = 'in/into' + امد = 'came'). بپوشید is past/preterite 'covered' (verb with the b- prefix used here as preterite/poetic form) and روی is the object 'face' (i.e. 'covered its face'). Word order places the incoming gloom first, then the action that affects the moon's face.
segments.s226-g491: تبیره is a noun (here acting as the thing that 'arose'); برآمد is the simple past 'arose/issued forth'. ز درگاه طوس is a prepositional phrase 'from the court of Tūs' with ز (poetic form of از) marking source and درگاه طوس showing genitive/possessive order (place + name). The clause has standard SVO/verb-last poetic order: [noun] + verb + prepositional origin.
segments.s226-g492: شد is the past of شدن 'became' and introduces a resultant state. از گرد اسپان زمین is a causal/agent phrase 'from the dust of the horses' (از = 'from', گرد = 'dust', اسپان = 'horses') placed before the patient زمین (ground) which is then described by ابنوس (here adjectival 'jet-black/ebony'). The final adjective ابنوس states the resulting quality after شدن.
segments.s226-g493: This line contains two coordinated clauses compressed together: زمین نیلگون شد ('the earth became blue/azure') — زمین (subject) + نیلگون (adjective) + شد (past 'became') — followed immediately by هوا پر ز گرد ('the air was full of dust') where هوا (subject) + پر (adjective 'full (of)') + ز گرد ('of dust') gives the content. The compact order and missing copula in the second clause are typical of epic poetic style.
segments.s226-g494: Here the verb بپوشید ('put on/donned') appears before the proper name رستم (subject) — a poetic inversion — followed by the object سلیح نبرد ('the war-armament/weapons of battle'), where سلیح = 'arms' and نبرد qualifies their use ('for battle'). The whole clause means 'Rostam put on/armed himself with battle-gear.'
segments.s226-g495: سوی میمنه ('on the right side') is a locative phrase; پور کشواد ('Keshvad's son') is the subject placed after the location for emphasis, followed by بود ('was') as the past copula. This word order (location + subject + بود) is common in epic diction to set the scene before identifying the person.
segments.s226-g496: که introduces a relative clause 'who/which', با is the preposition 'with', and the nouns جوشن و گرز ('cuirass and mace') are coordinated by و. پولاد follows گرز as a postpositive modifier meaning 'steel' ('a steel mace'). The clause closes with بود ('was') as the past copula describing the son.
segments.s226-g265: بیاری here functions like بِ یاری (with the aid/support of) placed before the name; it is an instrumental/associative phrase. افراسیاب is the proper name governed by that phrase, and آمدیم is the past simple, first-person plural (‘we came’), placed after the agent phrase in the normal S‑predicate order of older Persian.
segments.s226-g266: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and introduces the source phrase; دشت and دریای (دریا + genitive ـِ) are the nouns 'plain' and 'sea' respectively, with آب reinforcing 'water/sea'. The coordinating و links the two source phrases, and آمدیم is the repeated past plural verb for emphasis ('we came').
segments.s226-g267: بسی modifies باره: here it means 'many'/'numerous' and qualifies باره (بار) as 'loads/occasions'. The conjunction و links the following هدیه‌ها ('gifts/tributes'). یافتیم is the past plural 'we found/obtained' (perfect/simple past used to report the action).
segments.s226-g268: ز هر کشوری is a prepositional phrase 'from every country/land' (ز = 'from', هر = 'each/every', کشوری = 'country/land'). تیز is an adverb/adjectival adverb 'swift', placed before the verb بشتافتیم ('we hastened/rushed'), which is past plural; the adverb modifies the manner of that motion.
segments.s226-g269: بیک precedes مرد as an attributive/indefinite element (an older/poetic form related to a title/one); سگزی is a derogatory adjective 'dog-like'/'base' modifying مرد. The relative particle که introduces the clause 'who came to fight' (که آمد بجنگ): آمد is simple past 'he came' and بجنگ is an infinitive/purpose phrase 'to fight' (ب‑ + subjunctive/infinitive form).
segments.s226-g270: چرا is the interrogative 'why'; شد is past 'became' (resultative 'has become'); چنین 'so/thus' modifies the following predicate; بر شما marks the affected party 'upon you' and کار تنگ 'a narrow/difficult affair' is the nominal predicate ('the affair has become tight/difficult for you').
segments.s226-g271: ز یک مرد here marks the person implicated (literally 'for/of one man'); ننگست is an impersonal predicate 'it is shameful' (nang + -ast). گفتن سخن is an infinitival nominal phrase 'to say a word / to speak' functioning as the complement of ننگست (i.e., 'it is shameful to say such words').
segments.s226-g272: دگرگونه‌تر is the comparative/adverbial 'different / otherwise'; باید is the modal 'must/ought'; افگند is the classical verbal stem 'to cast/lay' (here used with باید as an obligation: 'one must cast/lay'); بن is the direct object 'root/foundation' (i.e., 'a different foundation must be laid'). Note the older, somewhat terse word order and the use of classical verbal forms.
segments.s226-g49: که is the conjunction 'that' linking this clause to what precedes. اکنون is an adverb 'now/at present'. برآمد is a past (perfective) verb 'arose/occurred' with بسی روزگار as its subject/complement; بسی (an emphatic/adverbial element) modifies روزگار ('times/events/fate'). Word order places the time adverb before the verb and the quantified noun phrase after it, a common classical arrangement.
segments.s226-g50: شنیدم is first-person singular past 'I heard' (the clitic -م marks the subject). بسی again emphasizes quantity (‘much/many’) and modifies پند ('advice, admonition'). آموزگار is the source/agent ('teacher'); here it stands after پند in a genitive/attributive relation ('the teacher's counsels' or 'counsels from the teacher').
segments.s226-g51: که again functions as the linking 'that'. شیون is the noun 'wailing/outcry'. نه is the negator placed before the verb to give 'no wailing rose'. برخاست is the past 'rose/arose', and از خان من is a prepositional phrase 'from my khan/house' indicating origin; خان here denotes a dwelling/inn/household associated with 'my'.
segments.s226-g52: همی is a frequentive/emphatic particle meaning 'continually' or 'indeed' in classical usage. آتش is 'fire' (literal or figurative passion). افروزد is a third-person singular present/habitual form 'kindles' (classical present form), with the subject understood from context; از جان من 'from my soul' locates the origin of that fire in the speaker's inner being.
segments.s226-g53: همی is emphatic/frequentive 'continuously'. خون خروشم is a possessive/construct phrase: خون 'blood' with خروشم ('my roar/tumult') where the suffix -م marks 'my' on the noun خروش (roar, tumult); together it pictures 'the roar of my blood'. بجای means 'in place of' or 'instead of', and سرشک is 'a teardrop', so the clause says the speaker's violent blood (or bloodshed) takes the place of tears.
segments.s226-g54: همیشه is the adverb 'always'. گرفتارم is the predicate 'I am afflicted/caught' with the first-person enclitic -م marking the subject. اندر is the classical preposition 'in/within' and پزشک is 'physician/healer'; together the phrase means the speaker is constantly in the physician's care or constantly afflicted and under the doctor's treatment.
segments.s226-g55: ازین (here written as از + ین) is 'from this' (از = from; ین = orthographic variant of این 'this'). کار is 'deed/affair'. بهر من (بهر = for/to; من = me) gives the beneficiary/affected party 'to me'. آمد is past 'came/happened' and گزند is the noun 'harm/damage' placed at the end as the affected event: 'from this deed harm came to me.'
segments.s226-g56: The clause begins with the negator نه (not) negating the verb گشت 'turned/became'. بر آرزو is a locative/directional phrase 'upon/according to (my) desire' (بر = on/upon; آرزو = wish). چرخ بلند 'the lofty Wheel' (چرخ = Wheel/Heaven/fate; بلند = lofty/high) is the subject — i.e. 'fortune did not turn in accordance with my wish.' Note the adjective بلند follows its noun in typical Persian order.
segments.s226-g465: مباد is an archaic optative/negative particle meaning “may it not be.” It governs the following noun phrase as the thing wished against: 'این جهان بی سرو تاج شاه' = 'this world without (a) cypress and the king's crown.' Word order is noun phrase after the optative particle.
segments.s226-g466: This is a jussive/subjunctive clause tied to the wish in مباد: تو (vocative/subject) + بادی is an archaic subjunctive/optative form meaning 'may you be' (not the noun 'wind'); همیشه is the temporal adverb 'always'; ورا پیش‌گاه is an adverbial locative 'there, before the court' (ورا = thither + پیش‌گاه = the court/audience). The verb (بادی) follows the subject in this older style.
segments.s226-g467: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying گفت. Here گفت is used as the past verb 'said' (the provided noun senses don't match), with the speaker name following in the inverted order: 'چنین گفت رستم' = 'thus said Rostam.' که introduces the subordinate clause 'شب تیره گشت' where شب (subject) + تیره (adjective) + گشت (past tense 'became/turned').
segments.s226-g468: ز is the poetic/archaic preposition 'from/because of,' marking cause. گفتارها is the plural 'utterances/speeches' (the cause), مغزها 'the brains/minds' is the affected subject, and خیره گشت is a compound predicate 'became astonished/stunned' (خیره here = 'astonished'). Word order presents cause first, then the experiencer, then the result verb.
segments.s226-g469: بباشیم is a 1st-person-plural jussive/subjunctive ('let us be/remain'), an older/formal hortative form. و joins the two clauses; تا نیم‌شب sets the time limit 'until midnight'; می‌خوریم is the present/habitual 'we eat'/'we will eat,' expressing continuing action up to that time.
segments.s226-g470: دگر نیمه is a temporal phrase 'the other half' (the remaining half of the night). تیمار لشکر is a genitive/construct: 'care/attending of the army.' بریم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/imperative 'let us take'/'we will take (charge of),' so the object phrase precedes the verb in classical word order.
segments.s226-g471: ببینیم is a hortative/jussive 'let us see' (1pl). تا here introduces the subordinate/purpose clause ('so that' / 'in order that') linking to what follows; کردگار جهان is a noun phrase 'the Creator of the world' (کردگار = God/Creator, modified by جهان).
segments.s226-g472: برین is the fused form of بر + ین (بر این) meaning 'on/with regard to this' or 'in this (matter)'; آشکارا is an adverb 'openly/evidently' modifying the clause; چه دارد نهان is a direct question/indirect interrogative 'what he has hidden' where دارد is present 'has/possesses' and نهان is the predicate adjective 'hidden.' Together this completes what we are to 'see.'
segments.s226-g105: This is a temporal clause: ز پیران ('from the elders') is a prepositional phrase giving source, چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when', بشنید is the past tense verb 'heard', and رستم is the subject placed after the verb (a common poetic/V‑S order). سخن is the object 'words/speech' that he heard.
segments.s226-g106: نه is the negative marker negating the whole following clause. بر آرزو is a prepositional phrase 'to/at the request/wish' (بر = 'toward/to', آرزو = 'wish/request'). پاسخ افگند is a classical/poetic past form (افگند ≈ افکند) literally 'cast/laid an answer' = 'gave an answer'; with نه it means 'did not answer'. بن appears as a short, poetic closing word here (not functioning as a normal noun in the clause) and is best read as a line‑ending particle or exclamatory residue rather than a syntactic object.
segments.s226-g107: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت here represents 'said' — note the lexeme list gives a nominal sense but it functions as the past verb 'گفت'). تا introduces the reported clause 'that/that I...', من is the subject 'I'. بدین is the fused preposition + demonstrative 'to/at this' (here segmented as ب + دین) and marks 'this battlefield' (بدین رزمگاه = 'in/for this battlefield').
segments.s226-g108: کمر بسته‌ام is a perfective/periphrastic expression 'I have girded my loins / I have bound my belt' (کمر = 'waist/girdle', بسته‌ام = 'I have bound'), i.e. 'I have prepared myself.' با + دلیران شاه is an instrument/association phrase 'with the king's warriors' (با = 'with', دلیران = plural noun 'brave ones/warriors', شاه = 'the king' modifying them).
segments.s226-g109: ندیدستم is the first‑person past negative perfective 'I did not see'. از تو 'from you' gives source/agent, به جز راستی is a restrictive phrase 'except truth' (به جز = 'except', راستی = 'truth/honesty'), so the segment states 'I saw nothing from you except truth.'
segments.s226-g110: ز ترکان همه = 'from the Turks, all' (ز = 'from', ترکان = 'the Turks', همه = 'all' or 'entirely'), and راستی خواستی is a past second‑person verb phrase 'you desired/asked for truth' (خواستی = 'you wanted/sought'). Put together: 'you demanded truth of all the Turks.'
segments.s226-g111: پلنگ این شناسد که is a verbless/nominal clause turned verbal in poetry: پلنگ 'the leopard' is the topic, این شناسد (poetic/archaic form of 'knows/recognizes') = 'knows this', and که introduces the content clause 'that ...' which will state what is known (پیکار و جنگ = 'battle and war'). Note the noun‑verb order reflects poetic inversions.
segments.s226-g112: نه خوبست is a simple predication 'are not good' (نه negates خوبست 'it/they are good'). و داند همی کوه و سنگ continues with a parallel clause: داند = 'know' (3rd person), همی is a small emphatic/poetic particle ('also/indeed'), and کوه و سنگ 'mountain and stone' are the subjects that 'know it as well' — i.e. even the mountain and stone know that war is not good.
segments.s226-g409: This is an imperative clause: 'یکی گوشه‌ای گیر' = 'take a corner / go to some secluded spot.' 'یکی' here means 'a certain' / 'some' modifying 'گوشه‌ای.' The verb 'گیر' (take/seize) follows the object phrase, a common flexible word order in older Persian. 'تا نزد شاه' is a purpose/result clause marker ('so that / until') plus the locative phrase 'نزد شاه' = 'in the presence of the king.'
segments.s226-g410: 'ز تو آشکارا نگردد گناه' is the purpose/result clause linked to the previous 'تا': 'so that the sin/guilt may not become apparent because of you.' 'ز تو' = 'because of/from you,' 'آشکارا' is an adverb 'openly/evidently,' and 'نگردد' is negative jussive/ subjunctive (classical form) third-person singular 'may not become/be revealed.'
segments.s226-g411: 'بگفتیم' is a past first‑person plural verb 'we said/said we.' The conjunction 'و' links to the next clause. 'پیران برین بازگشت' = 'the elders returned to this place/hither' — 'پیران' is the subject, 'بر' + 'ین' form the locative 'برین' meaning 'to here/unto this place,' and 'بازگشت' is the past verb 'returned.'
segments.s226-g412: This clause states an accompanying event: 'شب تیره با دیو انباز گشت' = 'the dark night went/returned with the demon Anbaz.' 'شب تیره' is the nominal subject ('dark night'), 'با دیو انباز' is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the demon Anbaz,' and 'گشت' is a past verb 'went/returned' (classical past form).
segments.s226-g413: 'هیونی فرستاد نزدیک شاه' = 'he sent a hyun/monster near the king.' 'هیونی' is the thing sent (a beast/monster), 'فرستاد' is the past verb 'sent,' and 'نزدیک شاه' is an adverbial phrase 'near the king.' The agent who sent is contextually supplied earlier; classical Persian often omits an overt subject if understood.
segments.s226-g414: The particle 'که' introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result: 'که لشکر برآرای کامد سپاه' — literally 'that the army may array/prepare, (and) the host comes.' 'لشکر' is subject 'army,' 'برآرای' is a verbal form meaning 'array/prepare/arrange' (here hortative/subjunctive sense), 'کامد' is an archaic present/potential 'comes/arrives,' and 'سپاه' functions as appositive or object ('the host'). The clause expresses intention: the sending was so the army/host would appear and be arrayed.
segments.s226-g415: 'تو گفتی' is second-person past 'you said' and 'که' introduces reported content. 'با ما نگفت این سخن' = 'he did not say this speech/word to us' — 'با ما' = 'to/with us,' 'نگفت' is negative past 'did not say,' and 'این سخن' is the object 'this statement.' Word order places the negative verb before the object, typical in classical Persian narration.
segments.s226-g416: This is a terse negative comment: 'نه سر بود ازان کار هرگز نه بن' = 'there was no head/beginning to that affair, nor ever a root.' The repeated 'نه ... نه ...' is a neither/nor construction. 'سر' here means 'origin/inner point,' 'بود' is past 'was,' 'ازان کار' = 'of that affair,' 'هرگز' = 'ever/never,' and 'بن' = 'root/foundation.' The order is somewhat inverted for poetic concision.
segments.s226-g153: بدانید is the imperative (2nd-person plural/formal) of دانستن 'know' — here 'know' or 'be aware'. کین is an archaic demonstrative 'this' placed before the noun phrase. شیر دل is a compound (شیر 'lion' + دل 'heart') meaning 'lion‑hearted' (brave). رستمست is an adjectival/possessive formation indicating relation to Rūstam ('is/of Rūstam' or 'Rūstam's').
segments.s226-g154: جهانگیر is an epithet/title meaning 'world‑conqueror' placed in apposition to the preceding noun phrase. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. از introduces lineage ('from/of') and تخمه here means 'seed/offspring, i.e. lineage'. نیرمست is used as the name/epithet of that lineage (literally printed here as a word whose ordinary dictionary sense doesn't fit), so از تخمه نیرمست = 'of the seed/lineage of Nīram(─)'.
segments.s226-g155: بزرگان is the plural noun 'great ones' or 'elders/chiefs'. و is 'and'. شیران is the plural 'lions' used figuratively here for 'brave men' or champions. زابلستان follows as a place name modifying those groups (’of Zabulistan’). The modifier follows the coordinated noun phrase.
segments.s226-g156: همه is the universal quantifier 'all' or 'everyone'. نامداران is a plural noun 'the famed/renowned ones'. کابلستان is the place-name 'Kabulistan' functioning as the domain or identifying region of those renowned men.
segments.s226-g157: چنو (classical spelling of such/like this) sets up a conditional/characterizing clause 'if/so/being such'. کینه‌ور is an adjectival predicate 'vindictive' or 'grudge-bearing'. باشد is the 3rd-person subjunctive/coprular form 'be' used after a conditional (here 'if he be/if he is such'). و links to the following nominal رهنمای 'guide/leader', so the clause reads 'if he is such a vindictive one and (is) a leader...'.
segments.s226-g158: سواران is the plural subject 'horsemen' or 'riders'. گیتی is a genitival/attributive 'of the world' specifying which riders. ندارند is the 3rd-person plural present 'they do not have' (negative of داشتن). پای literally 'foot' is used idiomatically as 'foothold/standing' — object of ندارند. Word order is Subject + modifier + verb + object.
segments.s226-g159: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a time clause. گودرز کشواد appears as a personal-name sequence (Goudarz Keshvad). و + چون before گیو gives a parallel 'and likewise' (چون = 'as/like'), linking گیو and طوس as additional names: 'and as Giv and Tous'. The list is a classical triad of heroes.
segments.s226-g160: بناکام is an adjectival predicate 'unsuccessful' (frustrated), and رزمی ('martial') narrows it to failure in warfare. بود is the copula 'was/were' linking the predicate to the (implied) subject (classical Persian can use a singular copula for a group or present a general state). با is the preposition 'with' and فسوس here denotes lament/complaint/sighing in context (an archaic/poetic word whose ordinary dictionary senses do not exactly match this idiomatic usage), so با فسوس = 'with lament/regret'.
segments.s226-g193: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' (frequent past narrative); بنزدیک خاقان is a prepositional/locative phrase 'near the khagan' with the adverbial بنزدیک before the noun; چو introduces a simile 'like/as'. گرد here is a poetic noun 'dust / dust-cloud' completing the simile 'like a dust-cloud'. Word order is verb — adverbial phrase — simile marker — simulated noun.
segments.s226-g194: پر از خون رخ is a Persian adjectival phrase where پر 'full (of)' takes a following از + noun to mark the content: 'face full of blood'. و links the two coordinated noun phrases; دیده پر آب زرد mirrors the first phrase: دیده 'eye(s)' + پر 'full (of)' + آب زرد 'yellowish water' (water described as زرد). The verb is omitted (poetic verbless clause) and the phrases describe his appearance.
segments.s226-g195: سراپرده او is the noun phrase 'his inner chamber' (سراپرده) with the pronoun او; پر از ناله is an adjectival phrase 'full of lament/moaning' (پر + از + ناله). دید is the past tense verb 'saw / beheld' placed at the end, so the whole segment reads 'He beheld his inner chamber full of wailing.'
segments.s226-g196: ز خون کشته is a genitive/ablative phrase 'from / of the blood of the slain' where کشته functions as 'the slain' (a noun). بر introduces the locus 'on/upon'; زعفران لاله is a poetic image 'tulips of saffron (i.e. tulips stained like saffron)' or 'tulips tinged with saffron-color'. The final دید repeats the verb 'he saw', so the line strings images seen by the speaker.
segments.s226-g197: ز introduces the source/association 'of' (poetic use). خویشان کاموس is a genitive phrase 'the kinsmen/relatives of Kamus' (کاموس here is a proper name; none of the provided common senses matches that use). چندی سپاه means 'a number of troops / some host' with چندی 'several/some' modifying سپاه 'army/host'.
segments.s226-g198: بنزدیک خاقان شده دادخواه is a clause in which بنزدیک خاقان 'near the khagan' is the locative phrase, شده is the perfect/passive participle 'have become' (here used as 'having become'), and دادخواه is a noun 'seeker of justice / petitioner / avenger'. Together they mean 'having come near the khagan, they became petitioners/seeking redress.'
segments.s226-g199: همی here is a frequentative/continuous adverb meaning 'kept / repeatedly'; گفت would normally be the past tense 'said' but the provided senses list does not include that meaning (so it's marked -1). هر کس که introduces a generic relative clause 'whoever / any man who'; افراسیاب is the proper name 'Afrasiab' filling the clause as topic or object depending on the reading.
segments.s226-g200: ازین پس is the adverbial phrase 'from now on' (از + ین (orthographic variant of 'این') + پس). بزرگی نبیند is a negative-future/subjunctive expression 'shall not see / will not behold greatness' with نبیند ('to see') used in the sense 'to behold/experience'. بخواب is the imperative 'go to sleep' used here as an interjection dismissing or ordering rest; in classical poetry such imperatives can conclude a statement or command.
segments.s226-g241: The pair of 'نه ... و نه ...' forms a correlative negation meaning 'neither ... nor ...'. Noun-noun sequences like 'چنگ پلنگ' and 'خرطوم پیل' express a possessive/genitive relation without the ezāfe (poetic compression): 'claw of the leopard' and 'trunk of the elephant'. 'و' simply links the two negative items inside the correlative structure.
segments.s226-g242: A repeat of the 'نه ... و نه ...' pattern: 'نه کوه بلند و نه دریای نیل' = 'neither a high mountain nor the Nile sea'. 'کوه بلند' is an adjective following the noun (typical Persian order), and 'دریای نیل' is 'the sea of the Nile' again with omitted ezāfe in poetic compression.
segments.s226-g243: 'بسندست' is an attributive/adverbial adjective meaning 'skillful / of sure hand' and stands before the accompanying phrase. 'با او' indicates accompaniment ('with him'), and 'به آوردگاه' gives the destination ('to the battlefield'). The clause is verbless in appearance but functions as a qualificative phrase describing action to follow.
segments.s226-g244: 'چو' here means 'when'. Two verbs follow in narrative sequence: 'آورد' (he brings) and 'گیرد' (he seizes/takes). Objects or pronouns are omitted (typical in Persian), so the clause reads 'when he brings (him/it), he seizes (him/it)'. 'به پیش سپاه' is a locative phrase 'in front of / before the army' modifying 'گیرد'.
segments.s226-g245: 'یکی' is an indefinite 'one / a certain (person)'. 'رخش دارد' means 'has (or bears) his steed' (straightforward possessive verb construction). The locative adverbial 'بزیر اندرون' ('downward inside') modifies how the steed is held or positioned; word order (noun + verb + locative) is normal in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s226-g246: The clause begins with the subordinating 'که' (that / so that / as), followed by 'گویی' used idiomatically as 'as if / it seems' (note: that particular pragmatic sense is not among the supplied sense-entries, see senses mapping). 'روان شد' is a verbal phrase 'became moving' or 'took motion' (simple past narrative), and the final 'که بیستون' evokes Bisotun — the second 'که' introduces the simile/comparison target. Overall: 'so that it seemed as if Bisotun moved.'
segments.s226-g247: 'کنون' = 'now' sets the temporal frame. 'روز خیره' is a noun + adjective phrase ('a day of wonder / an astonishing day'); poetic word-order and adjective choice convey emotional coloring. 'نباید شمرد' is an impersonal modal construction: 'نباید' (one should not / must not) + bare verb stem 'شمرد' (to count/consider), meaning 'one should not count/consider (it)'.
segments.s226-g248: The clause begins with 'که' ('for / that'), introducing the reason or explanation. 'دیدند' is past tense 'they saw'; 'هر کس' = 'every/each person'; 'ازو' = 'from him / of him'; and 'دستبرد' here means an act of seizure/encroachment ('laid hands on / plundered'). The sentence thus gives the cause: they saw that everyone was seizing from him.
segments.s226-g169: همی before a past-tense verb gives a habitual/continuous sense: همی گفتم = 'I kept saying' or 'I kept on saying.' این introduces the following noun phrase, شوم is an adjective ('ill-omened, accursed') placed before the noun بیداد ('injustice/oppression'), and را is the definite/object marker attaching to the whole noun phrase.
segments.s226-g170: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that…'). چندین is a quantifier ('several/many a') modifying مدار; مدار here means a circuit/course or successive revolutions. آتش و باد is a coordinated nominal phrase ('fire and wind'), and the trailing را again marks the phrase as definite/object (poetic word order leaves the clause open to continue).
segments.s226-g171: که again links this clause to the previous. روزی here functions adverbially ('one day,' i.e. at some future time). شوی is second-person singular present/subjunctive of شدن ('you will become/you will be'), ناگهان is an adverb 'suddenly,' and سوخته is a past-participle adjective 'burned/scorched' describing the state.
segments.s226-g172: This short verbless sequence is poetic listing: خرد سوخته = 'wisdom scorched' (خرد as noun 'wisdom' modified by سوخته), چشم دل دوخته = two nouns in juxtaposition where چشم = 'eye' and دل = 'heart', both followed by دوخته ('sewn/stitched' used metaphorically). The lack of a linking verb is typical in Persian poetry, producing a compressed, descriptive effect.
segments.s226-g173: نکرد is past negative of کردن ('did not do/did not carry out'). آن + adjective-noun جفاپیشه ('that oppressive/cruel one') is the subject, and فرمان من ('my command') is the direct object (possessive relation expressed without ezāfe in poetic diction). Word order is subject–object–verb (typical Persian), with the verb fronted here as a negative past.
segments.s226-g174: نه ... نه ... is a paired negative ('neither ... nor ...'). فرمان این نامدار انجمن = 'the command/order of this famed assembly' where این qualifies the following noun phrase; نامدار modifies انجمن ('renowned/illustrious council/assembly'). The phrase contrasts with فرمان من earlier.
segments.s226-g175: بکند is a poetic verb form (here third-person) meaning 'he caused/removed' or 'he did' and introduces the action. این گرانمایگان ('these distinguished/illustrious persons') is the definite object marked by را, and ز جای literally means 'from/place' = 'from their posts/positions.' The verb + را + ز جای conveys 'tore them from their places.'
segments.s226-g176: نزد with this construction means 'in the presence of' or 'before' (not merely 'near'). با introduces accompaniment or instrumentality ('with/by'), here followed by two coordinated adjectives/nouns دلیر و خردمند ('the brave and the wise'). رای follows as 'counsel/opinion/plan,' so the whole phrase says the action happened despite or even before the brave and wise counsel.
segments.s226-g89: به here introduces a wish/blessing (an optative use: 'may ... be / let ... be'). پیروزگر is a noun 'victor' or 'victorious one' placed after به; بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'upon you' with the preposition بر before the pronoun (تو) and the poetic vocative particle ای attached (تو‌ای = 'O you'). پهلوان is a vocative noun 'hero/champion'. The overall order is optative particle + predicate + prepositional phrase + vocative.
segments.s226-g90: که introduces the following clause with a purposive/circumstantial sense ('so that/that'); از من is a prepositional phrase meaning 'away/from me'; نباشی is the negative second-person subjunctive/optative ('may you not be' / 'do not be'), here expressing a wish that the addressee avoid a state; خلیده‌روان is a compound adjective 'withdrawn-souled, contemplative' describing the state to be avoided. Word order is conjunction → prepositional phrase → negative-subjunctive verb → predicate adjective.
segments.s226-g91: ز (poetic variant of از) governs the following noun phrase and means 'toward/from' (here 'toward'). خویشان من = 'my relatives' (possessive expressed by following personal pronoun). بد نداری is an expression meaning 'ill-will / bad feeling' or literally 'lack (of good)'; here it functions as 'do not have ill-will' (in Classical usage the verb can be implied). نهان is an adverb 'secretly/hidden' placed after the clause it modifies.
segments.s226-g92: براندیشی is an imperative/subjunctive infinitival idea 'consider/reflect on' (in classical Persian it can be used as a verb form meaning 'think upon'); از here is the preposition 'of/on/about' used with the object; کردگار جهان is a genitive construction 'Creator of the world' (کردگار = God/the Maker, جهان = the world). The adverb نهان from the previous segment scopes over this verb: 'secretly reflect on...'.
segments.s226-g93: بروشن روان is a poetic compound adjective phrase ('bright-souled' or 'clear/bright of spirit') placed before the proper name as an epithet; سیاوش is the vocative proper name. که here is causal/explanatory 'for'/'because' introducing the clause that follows. Word order: epithet → name (vocative) → conjunction.
segments.s226-g94: This clause states a comparative predication with an omitted copula (poetic ellipsis of هست 'is'). مرگ is the subject 'death'; مرا is a dative 'to me' (literally 'death, to me'); خوشتر is the comparative adjective 'more pleasant / dearer'; از introduces the standard Persian comparative ('than'); جوشن و تیغ و ترگ lists the things being compared (armor, sword, rags). The small form م is a poetic remnant of the copula (est) and the object marker را appears as clitic; overall the verb 'is' is implied rather than written.
segments.s226-g95: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونکه (an archaic/poetic compound meaning roughly 'now that / at this time') sets the temporal/circumstantial frame; جنگی بود is a past-tense or subjunctive copular phrase 'there were a battle' (بود here functions as the past/irrealis copula: 'were'); هم‌گروه is an adjective/adverbial 'together/with the group' or 'cohort', qualifying the situation. The clause as a whole gives a hypothetical: 'If now a battle were (to occur)...'.
segments.s226-g96: تلی is a noun 'a heap/pile'; کشته is in the sense 'the slain' (a noun meaning those killed). بینی is the second-person present-subjunctive/indicative of 'to see' or the archaic/poetic form 'you would see' (here 'you see / you would see'); ببالای is a compound prepositional form 'on the top of / up on' and کوه is the object 'mountain'. Word order is conditional result → visual verb → location phrase.
segments.s226-g161: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' placed before the noun phrase; 'ترکان گنهکار' is a noun + adjective phrase ('the sinful Turks'). 'خواهد' is a modal/future auxiliary 'will' that here anticipates the verb in the next segment; 'همی' is an archaic continuous/emphatic particle (classical Persian) attached to the verbal complex and marks ongoing/regular action.
segments.s226-g162: 'دل' is the subject ('heart, sympathy'); 'از' governs the following noun phrase 'بیگناهان' ('the innocent') and marks separation/withdrawal. 'بکاهد' is the present/future stem (ب‑ + کاهَد) meaning 'will lessen/withdraw' (classical present); the verb takes 'دل' as what is diminished. The final 'همی' repeats the old continuous/emphatic particle attached to the verb.
segments.s226-g163: Initial 'که' is the complementizer introducing an assertive clause ('that'); 'دانی' is 2nd-person present 'you know' (used rhetorically as 'as you know' or 'it is known that'). The second 'که' begins the embedded clause 'ایدر گنهکار نیست' ('here there is no sinner'); 'نیست' negates existence following the noun, typical classical word order.
segments.s226-g164: 'دل شاه' is a juxtaposed possessive construction ('the king's heart'); 'ازو' ('from him' / 'about him') marks the object/target of feeling. 'پر ز تیمار' is an adjective phrase 'full of care' where 'پر' means 'full (of)' and 'ز' introduces the content; 'نیست' is the copular negation 'is not' applying to the whole predicate.
segments.s226-g165: 'نگه کن' is an imperative ('look!' / 'watch!') formed from noun + imperative verb 'کن'. The particle 'که' introduces the subordinate clause describing what to watch: 'این بوم ویران شود' where 'این' = 'this', 'بوم' = 'land/homeland' and 'ویران شود' is the present/subjunctive/future passive-like form 'become ruined' (شد/شود used for 'become' or 'will be').
segments.s226-g166: 'بکام' is an archaic fused prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'to the desire/pleasure of'; it modifies the following predicate. 'دلیران ایران' is a genitival noun phrase 'the brave of Iran' (brave men/heroes of Iran). 'شود' again is 'becomes/will be' so the whole phrase means 'it will become to the liking/pleasure of Iran's brave.'
segments.s226-g167: The paired negator 'نه ... نه' ('neither ... nor') starts the list: 'پیر و جوان' ('old and young'). 'ماند' is the verb 'remain' and 'ایدر' ('here') marks location; classical word order places the verb before the locative. The final 'نه شاه' continues the correlative negation ('nor the king').
segments.s226-g168: This segment continues the 'neither ... nor' list by ellipsis of the verb (the verb 'remain' from the previous segment is understood). Items are linked by 'و' ('and') between each noun: 'گنج و سپاه' (treasure and army) and 'تخت و کلاه' (throne and hat/crown). The repeated 'نه' at the start of the segment signals continued negation.
segments.s226-g201: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. کین 'vengeance' is the noun being discussed. پی افگند is a poetic combination: پی 'in pursuit/after' + افگند (past-stem of افکندن) 'cast/set', here together indicating the idea of setting vengeance in motion or 'pursuing vengeance'. کش is the agentive element (often forming nouns like '—kesh', 'one who does X') placed after the verb-phrase for metrical/poetic reasons; نیست is the existential 'is not'. مرد is 'man' (here 'a heroic man' or 'a valiant man'). The whole segment literally asks why there does not exist a man who is an executor/pursuer of vengeance.
segments.s226-g202: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that' modifying مرد. آورَد + سازَد are two verbs piled together: آورَد here carries the sense 'bring/cause' and سازَد 'make/do' (both present/future/hortative sense in classical diction), together amounting to 'who will bring about' or 'who will carry/prep[are]'. بروز is the occasion/day (here 'the day/occasion of'), and نبرد is 'battle'. Grammatically, the clause describes what such a man would do: bring (cause) [it] on the day of battle.
segments.s226-g203: سپاه 'army' is the direct noun. کشانی is derived from کشاندن ('to bring/drag/lead') and functions adjectivally/verbally here ('that we will lead/send'), so the phrase means 'let us send/lead an army'. سوی 'toward' marks the direction, چین is 'China', and شویم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative of شدن 'to become/go' used here as a hortative 'let us go/become' — together expressing 'let us move/send an army toward China.'
segments.s226-g204: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. دیده 'eye' with پر 'full (of)' + آب 'water' is a standard poetic expression for 'eyes full of tears' (آب = tears here, figurative). و links the next predicate; باکین ('ba-kīn') is an adjective meaning 'filled with/ready for vengeance' or 'vengeful', and شویم again is the hortative 'let us become' (we may become). So the clause says 'let every eye be filled with tears and let us become vengeful.'
segments.s226-g205: ز ... و ز ... is a poetic repetition meaning 'from X and from Y'. چین 'China' and بربر (here used as 'barbarians') name the lands/peoples. سپاه 'army' is the object to be brought, and آوریم is 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive 'we will bring' / 'let us bring' indicating intention or hortatory future action.
segments.s226-g206: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause. کاموس here is a proper name (a person to be brought) and does not match the dictionary senses given, so it is treated as a name. را is the definite object marker attaching to کاموس. کینه‌خواه is a compound meaning 'seeker/agent of vengeance' or 'avenger' (an active, vengeful figure). آوریم repeats the hortative/intentional 'we will bring' and applies to bringing کاموس in the role of 'avenger.'
segments.s226-g207: ز introduces sources ('from'). بزگوش and سگسار are descriptive epithets for peoples/groups ('goat-eared' and 'dog-mauled' respectively) used poetically to name hostile peoples. مازندران is the region Mazandaran (here the mythical realm). The sequence lists the origins of the troops to be summoned.
segments.s226-g208: کس here means 'people/some person(s)' to be brought. آریم is the verbal form meaning 'we will bring' (1st-person-plural hortative/optative); the lemma senses provided did not include 'we bring', so its sense here is not in the supplied list. با 'with' introduces the weapon, گرزهای is the plural 'maces' (گرز) with the plural suffix -ها (here written together), and گران is the adjective 'heavy' (or 'valuable/weighty') modifying گرزهای. The clause thus says we will bring people with heavy maces.
segments.s226-g305: بگفت is the past third-person singular 'said' (the b- prefix is an archaic/poetic form). آنچ (= آن‌چه) is a relative pronoun 'what/that which' and takes the clause شنگل بگفت as its complement, so the phrase means 'Bahman said that which Shangal said.' Word order places the reported clause after the relative.
segments.s226-g306: گشت is the past 'became/was made'. سپه ('the host/army') is the subject; با او 'with him' is the comitative phrase, and به پیگار 'to/for battle' is an adverbial phrase of purpose. جفت here functions as an adjectival/predicative 'matched/paired'. The whole clause means 'the army became matched with him for battle.'
segments.s226-g307: The nominal غمی + verbal گشت is an impersonal/predicative construction meaning 'sorrow came to/attacked (someone)'. هومان follows as the experiencer (Hooman). ازان کار سخت gives the cause/ground 'from that hard/severe affair.'
segments.s226-g308: برآشفت is a past intransitive 'became angry/was upset'. با introduces the target of his anger ('with Shangal'); شوربخت is a descriptive epithet 'wretched/unfortunate' modifying شنگل. The sequence therefore reads 'he became angry with the unfortunate Shangal.'
segments.s226-g309: به پیران is a dative/prepositional phrase 'to the elders'. چنین گفت is the common formula 'he said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so'). کز آسمان begins the reported content with کز (= که + ز) functioning as 'that/from', introducing the subordinate clause referring to 'the heavens.'
segments.s226-g310: گذر نیست is an existential negative 'there is no passage/sign'. تا here introduces the subordinate limit/content 'to/that' and links to بر چه گردد زمان (literally 'on what the time will turn'), where گردد is the classical third-person singular present/subjunctive 'become/turn'. The clause asks/observes that no heavenly sign shows when time will change.
segments.s226-g311: بیامد is the past 'came'. The noun بره ('lamb') is used as the subject/agent here (a metaphorical epithet for a young or ingenuous person). پیش کلباد means 'before/in the presence of Kalbad'. گفت is the speech verb 'said' (the provided senses list does not include this verbal sense, so it is treated as the reporting verb).
segments.s226-g312: که introduces the content clause. شنگل is the subject. مگر here functions as a negative/emphatic particle ('is not.../surely not' in context) and با خرد نیست جفت means 'is not a match with wisdom' — جفت used predicatively as 'a match/peer'. The clause therefore states that Shangal is not, in any case, a match for reason/wisdom.
segments.s226-g329: چو here is the simile marker 'like/as' placed before the phrase. گرگین is a proper name (Gorgin) used before its epithets. کارآزموده is a compound participial adjective 'tested/experienced' that follows the name and qualifies the following noun. سوار is the head noun 'rider/horseman.' The order (name + adjective + noun) is poetic but straightforward: 'like Gorgin, an experienced rider.'
segments.s226-g330: چو again marks a simile. بیژن is the proper name Bijan. فروزنده is an active participle/adjective 'illuminating, radiant, one who lights' that attributes brightness or heroic radiance to Bijan. کارزار is the noun 'battle/field' which is being modified by فروزنده (i.e. 'battle‑illuminating' or 'who lights the battlefield').
segments.s226-g331: تهمتن is the name/epithet (Tahmatan) and functions as the subject. چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying گفت. گفت is the past tense verb 'said/spoke' (here the past of گفتن); با + بخردان is a prepositional phrase 'with the wise ones' indicating accompaniment. Note: the provided noun senses for گفت do not include the verbal sense 'said', so the verb function is what is used here.
segments.s226-g332: هشیوار و بیدار are coordinated adjectives 'alert and wakeful' that qualify the following nominal expression. دل here forms the compound 'بیدار دل' (wakeful‑hearted / with an awake heart), i.e. describing inner attentiveness. موبدان is the plural of موبد 'mōbed' (Zoroastrian priest) and is the head noun modified by the preceding adjectives.
segments.s226-g333: کسی را که introduces a relative clause 'someone whom/that...'; را is the object/definiteness marker attached to کسی. که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing the clause. یزدان is 'God' as the agent. کند here is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive form of کردن 'to make/do' and together with نیکبخت ('fortunate') forms the predicate 'makes (him) fortunate.' The clause as a whole means 'someone whom God makes fortunate.'
segments.s226-g334: سزاوار is an adjective 'deserving/fit.' باشد is the present/subjunctive (optative) form of بودن 'be' used here with a wish/entitlement sense 'should be / ought to be.' ورا (ورا = به + را) is a dative/indirect object 'to him' (i.e. 'for him'). تاج و تخت are the coordinated nouns 'crown and throne' that are the things of which he is deserving. Word order places the predicate 'سزاوار باشد' before the indirect object and the items deserved.
segments.s226-g335: جهانگیر is used as an epithet/adjective 'world‑conquering' (not here a personal name). و links the coordinated adjective پیروز 'victorious.' باشد is the optative/subjunctive 'may he be' or 'let him be.' بجنگ is an adverbial/locative expression formed with the preposition/prefix ب‑ + جنگ 'in battle' (literally 'in war' / 'in fight'), indicating the sphere in which he should be victorious.
segments.s226-g336: نباید is an impersonal negative 'must not / should not.' که introduces the subordinate clause. بیند is the present/subjunctive of دیدن 'see/experience' (here 'experience' or 'suffer'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' خود is reflexive 'his own / himself' indicating source. زور is 'strength/force' and چنگ is a metaphorical noun 'claw/talon' (here used figuratively for violent force). The phrase 'بیند ز خود زور چنگ' therefore literally means 'experience/see the force of a claw from his own side,' i.e. 'should not suffer a claw's force from himself/one of his own' — the segment orders the source (ز خود) before the object (زور چنگ).
segments.s226-g273: اگر introduces a protasis ('if'). گرد here is a noun meaning 'circle/around' (the company or throng around someone) and takes کاموس as its possessor; را is the object/definiteness marker attached to کاموس. ز + و (here written as separate words) functions poetically as 'from/at' (i.e. 'from that') and زمان 'time' completes the temporal phrase 'at that time'. Word order places the conditional circumstantial phrase up front.
segments.s226-g274: بیامد is used in a conditional sense here ('may come' / 'should come') rather than plain past. نباید is an impersonal modal meaning 'one must not / ought not to'. شدن is the infinitive 'to become' used here as the complement of نباید. بدگمان is an adjective 'suspicious' that completes the predication 'become suspicious.'
segments.s226-g275: سپیده‌دمان is an adverbial temporal phrase 'at daybreak' placed first for emphasis. گرزها is the plural object 'the clubs/maces' and برکشیم is a hortative/first-person-plural present subjunctive ('let us raise/lift'), with the prefix بر- marking the direction 'up/on'.
segments.s226-g276: وزین here qualifies دشت (literary adjective, 'this/weighty/dignified plain') and دشت is the location. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/straight on' and سراندر 'throughout/over' indicates the span; کشیم is a 1st-plural hortative verb 'let us draw/advance' (classical poetic usage meaning 'let us move/charge across'). The verb returns the hortative sense begun in the previous segment.
segments.s226-g277: هوا را uses را to mark 'the air' as definite object. چو 'like/as' introduces a simile; ابر بهاران literally 'spring clouds' is the image, and کنیم is the 1st-plural hortative 'let us make/do', so the clause means 'let us make the air like spring clouds.'
segments.s226-g278: بریشان = بر ایشان 'upon them' (the enemy). یکی here means 'together/as one' and modifies the ensuing action. تیرباران is a compound noun 'a shower/volley of arrows.' کنیم (same root as before) is the hortative 'let us make/rain' — together: 'let us rain a volley of arrows upon them.'
segments.s226-g279: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. گرد in this context means 'dust' (the cloud kicked up by horsemen). سواران is the plural 'horsemen/riders'. و links the two harmful causes, and زخم تبر 'the wound/blow of the axe' names the second cause; the whole is an ablative listing the instruments/effects that will befall people.
segments.s226-g280: نباید is the impersonal modal 'there should not/ought not'. که here introduces a relative/clausal complement ('that / who'); داند is the present tense of 'to know' used in sense 'to perceive/recognize'. کس 'anyone' is the indefinite subject of that clause. از پای سر is a fixed classical phrase 'from foot to head' (meaning 'from head to foot' or 'entirely'); grammatically it completes what 'کس داند' would or would not be able to 'know/recognize.'
segments.s226-g425: سر بخت کاموس is a nominal subject phrase with an epithet: Sarbakht (سر بخت) + the proper name کاموس. برگشته is a past participle/adverbial participle 'having turned' that precedes the finite past verb دید, so برگشته دید = 'having turned, (he) saw.'
segments.s226-g426: This clause gives what he saw. بخم کمند is an archaic phrase meaning 'in the bend/loop of the snare' (بخم modifying کمند). اندرش = اندر + ـش 'in it' (the snare). کشته is a past-participle adjective 'slain', and دید is the past-tense verb 'saw' taking the clause 'اندرش کشته' as its content.
segments.s226-g427: در آشتی is a prepositional phrase 'for/in reconciliation/peace'. جوید is a third-person present/present-habitual verb 'he seeks'; اکنون همی is an adverbial/temporal phrase 'now (indeed/still)', with همی functioning as an emphatic/continuous particle common in Classical Persian.
segments.s226-g428: نشستن بهامون is an infinitival phrase 'to sit with/us' (به + امون = 'with/for us'). نیارد here functions as a negative/future-like verb meaning roughly 'he will not come' or 'he does not bring himself' (an archaic form); همی again is emphatic. Together: 'he will not come to sit with us.'
segments.s226-g429: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. داند is third-person present 'he knows/sees'; که introduces the content clause. تنگ اندر آمد is a small clause 'entered (into) a narrow (place)' (تنگ = 'narrow' and اندر آمد = 'came in, entered'). نشیب is a noun 'descent/defile' completing the idea of a narrow place.
segments.s226-g430: بکار آورد is a verb phrase 'he made use of / employed' (بکار = 'to use', آورد = 'brought/used'). بند و رنگ و فریب are a coordinated list of instruments/means: بند (fetters/constraint), رنگ (guise/appearance/disguise), and فریب (deception), all objects of بکار آورد.
segments.s226-g431: گنهکار is a substantive 'the guilty/sinful man' or 'sinner'. با گنج و با خواسته is a coordinate prepositional phrase 'with treasure and with a request/entreaty' (listing the resources or bribes he uses).
segments.s226-g432: که introduces a reported clause. گفته‌ست is a perfect/passive-like impersonal 'it is said / he has said'. پیش is 'before / in the presence of', آرم here is an older form meaning 'I will bring / I may bring' (a 1st-person verbal form used in reported speech), and آراسته is a past-participle/adjective 'adorned/arrayed' describing what he promised to bring.
segments.s226-g257: مگر introduces a rhetorical question/contrasting suggestion ('shouldn't we / why not'). زین بلا is a causal/ablative phrase meaning 'from this calamity' (زین = 'from this' in classical usage; here listed separately from ز + این). سوی کشور is a directional phrase 'toward the country/realm.' شویم is the 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive of شدن used optatively/imperatively: 'let us go/turn' or 'shall we become/turn'. Overall word order is: modal question (مگر) + cause (زین بلا) + direction (سوی کشور) + verb (شویم).
segments.s226-g258: اگر چند functions concessively ('even if / although'); چند here is classical and means 'though / albeit' (a concessive particle). با بخت لاغر is an ablative/with‑phrase 'with lean/poor fortune' — a poetic way to say 'by unlucky fate.' شویم repeats the 1pl subjunctive/potential of شدن: 'we may become' or 'we may be.' The clause concedes a possible adverse outcome to justify turning to the realm.
segments.s226-g259: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of,' introducing the cause. پیران غمی is a genitive/construct phrase 'the elders' grief' (پیران = elders, غمی = grief). گشت is the past of گشتن 'became' (turned into). خاقان is the subject 'the khaghan' and چین functions as the predicate noun 'wrinkle(s)' — idiomatically 'the khaghan became wrinkled.' Word order puts cause first, then the verb and predicate.
segments.s226-g260: بسی here is an adverbial intensifier 'much / greatly' (classical usage). یاد کردن is a light-verb construction: یاد (remembrance) + کرد (past of کردن) = 'he remembered.' از marks the object/source ('of / concerning the Creator'), and جهان‌آفرین is the object 'the world‑creator / Creator.' The clause therefore says he remembered the Creator greatly.
segments.s226-g261: بدو = 'to him / unto him' (prepositional phrase marking the hearer). گفت is the verb 'said' (3rd sg past) introducing direct speech. ما را is the object/what is being inquired about ('our condition' or 'for us'); کنون 'now' places time. چیست is the copula question 'what is' (چی + ست) and روی is the predicate noun 'face/condition' used metaphorically for 'state.' The order reflects classical Persian: indirect object + verb + object/time + interrogative predicate.
segments.s226-g262: چو آمد is a temporal subordinate clause 'when / as (it) came' (چو = when; آمد = came). سپاهی is the noun 'army/warrior contingent' and جنگجوی is the modifying noun 'warrior' (چنین qualifies as 'such'). چنين precedes جنگجوی here as a demonstrative/adverbial 'such' — together: 'when such a warrior army arrived.' The clause supplies the circumstance for the preceding question.
segments.s226-g263: چنین 'thus' introduces reported speech. گفت is 'said' (verb) with شنگل as the speaker. که‌ای is an emphatic/vocative particle (classical contraction of که + ای) addressing someone: 'O ...!' سرفراز is the vocative epithet 'proud/exalted/victorious one.' Word order: adverb (چنین) + verb + name + vocative phrase.
segments.s226-g264: چه is an interrogative particle 'what/why' here functioning as 'why.' باید is the modal 'must / ought to.' کشیدن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to draw out / to prolong' (here used impersonally after باید: 'one must draw out'). سخنها is the plural 'words / speeches' and دراز 'long' is the adjective placed after the noun (classical order). The clause asks why lengthy speeches should be drawn out.
segments.s226-g433: ببینی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive with the predictive/epic ب‑ prefix (here: “you will see” / “you shall see”). که introduces a content clause (“that”/“when …”). چون here means “when/as.” بردمد is an archaic/poetic perfect/preterite (برآمد) meaning “arose/sounded,” and زخم کوس is a genitive noun phrase “the blow/beat of the drum” (زخم + کوس = drum‑strike), with the noun phrase following the verb as in classical Persian.
segments.s226-g434: بجنگ functions adverbially/verbally as ‘into battle’ or ‘to battle’ (ب‑ + جنگ). اندر آید is a verb phrase ‘comes in/enters’ (آید = 3rd sg. present/simple). The subject سپهدار طوس (the commander Tūs) is postposed after the verb, a common VSO/order in older Persian.
segments.s226-g435: سپهدار is the predicate noun ‘commander’; پیران here stands in apposition as a proper name (Pīrān) and is linked by the copula بود (past of بودن, “was”). پیش رو is an adverbial phrase ‘in front’ (پیش + رو) placed after the copula.
segments.s226-g436: که is a relative pronoun ‘who/that’ referring back to Pīrān. جنگ آورد is a verbal phrase where آورد is past tense ‘brought/caused’ — here habitual/past‑recounting ‘would bring about/lead battle.’ هر زمان means ‘whenever’/‘each time,’ and نوبنو (نو + be‑/poetic form) is an adverb ‘anew’ modifying آورد.
segments.s226-g437: دروغست is a copular clause ‘it/is a lie’ (دروغ + -ست ‘is’). یکسر is an adverb ‘utterly/entirely.’ همه گفت اوی is a noun phrase ‘all his sayings’ or ‘everything he said’ (همه = all; گفت used as ‘saying/utterance’; اوی = his/that he), so the whole clause means ‘all he said was pure lie.’
segments.s226-g438: نشاید is an impersonal/modal negative ‘it is not fitting/it should not be’ used to assert impossibility. جز از = ‘except from/of.’ اهرمن is Ahriman/the devil. جفت اوی = ‘his pair/companion’ (جفت used as ‘mate/companion’ with اوی ‘his’), so the clause says he can be nothing but the devil’s companion.
segments.s226-g439: اگر بشنوی is a conditional ‘if you hear’ (ب‑ prefix + شنوی, 2nd‑person present/subjunctive). سر بسر is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning ‘throughout/from beginning to end’ (i.e., ‘completely’) modifying پند من (‘my advice’). Word order places the conditional clause before the imperative.
segments.s226-g440: نگه کن is an imperative formed by the noun/gerund نگه ‘keeping/watch’ plus the light verb کن ‘do!’ meaning ‘keep/watch/attend to’ (i.e., ‘take care of’). ببهرام is the dative/beneficiary phrase ‘for Bahram’ (بـ + Bahram), and فرزند من is an appositive ‘my son,’ specifying who Bahram is.
segments.s226-g137: چو here functions as a comparative/contrastive marker 'as/compared to'. The sequence lists three proper names joined by و (and) in the normal Persian coordination order: 'Hūmān and Kalbad and Farshīdvard'.
segments.s226-g138: کجا هست is a locative/question phrase 'where is/where stands'. گودرز is the person being asked about; زیشان (= زِ ایشان) attached indicates relation 'of them / among them' (i.e. compared with them). بدرد is used predicatively 'to be of use / to avail' so the whole asks where/if Goudarz is of any use compared to those three.
segments.s226-g139: همه is the subject 'everyone'; زین (this saddle/trappings) is the thing being counted; شمارند is a plural present/past-subjunctive-form 'they count/reckon' (here narratively 'they call it'). و این روی نیست = 'and this face/appearance does not exist' — این modifies روی (appearance/face) and نیست negates it.
segments.s226-g140: مر is the older form of 'مرا'/'به من' meaning 'to me' or 'for me' (benefactive). این آب را is the object 'this water' (را marks definiteness/object). در جهان جوی نیست literally 'there is no stream in the world' — جوی here 'stream/source' and نیست negates existence.
segments.s226-g141: مرا (here shown split as م + را) marks the beneficiary/accusative 'me / for me'. چاره خوی‌ش is 'one's own remedy' formed by خوی + ش (possessive enclitic) after چاره. باید گرفت is a necessity construction: باید + past stem (گرفت) gives 'must be taken' / 'one must take'.
segments.s226-g142: ره جست is a compound meaning 'the way/path of seeking' (ره 'way' + جست from جستن 'to seek'); را marks it as the object/topic. پیش here means 'before/first, beforehand' and precedes the modal باید گرفت 'must be taken' — the word order places the adverbial 'first' before the modal verb phrase.
segments.s226-g143: بدو = 'to him' (داور form 'be + to' used as dative 'unto him'); پیران is the subject 'the elders'. گفت is the verb 'said' (past). که‌ای here is the classical connective + vocative (که + ای) introducing direct speech or address: 'that (they said), "O..."'.
segments.s226-g144: همیشه 'always'; جوان باش is an imperative 'be young/ever-youthful' (باش imperative of بودن). و links the two wishes; روشن‌روان is a compound adjective 'bright‑souled / light‑spirited' describing the desired state — here it functions as a parallel predicative wish.
segments.s226-g337: This clause uses a poetic, inverted word order: ز (from) + یزدان (God) + بود (was) + زور ما (our might) = “Our might was from God.” The short question خود کییم follows as a separate, elliptical interrogative: خود (ourselves) + کییم (who-are-we?, archaic 1st/1st‑person‑plural interrogative form) — a rhetorical “who are we?”
