segments.s226-g338: بدین here is a prepositional phrase meaning “in/with this” (ب = to/into; combined as poetic بدین = “in this”), followed by تیره خاک اندرون = “gloomy/dark earth within.” The final بر چییم is a rhetorical question: بر (prepositional/relational particle) + چییم (what-are-we?, interrogative form) — literally, “what are we upon/in (this)?” The whole clause asks our purpose/being within this dark earth.
segments.s226-g339: بباید is an impersonal modal “one must / it is necessary.” کشیدن is an infinitive/gerund here (literally “to draw/harbor”) used with the noun گمان (suspicion) to mean “to harbor/hold suspicion.” از بدی is a prepositional phrase “from/of evil,” so the clause means “one must cast off (the) suspicion arising from evil.”
segments.s226-g340: ره ایزدی is a noun phrase “the divine way/course.” باید again gives necessity (“must / it is necessary”), and بخردی is a noun meaning “wisdom / prudence.” The clause is elliptical: “[Follow] the divine way — it must [be followed] and [requires] prudence.” The copula/verb is omitted as a classical poetic economy.
segments.s226-g341: که begins a causal/explanatory clause “because/that.” گیتی نماند همی combines noun + verb + adverb: گیتی (the world) + نماند (does not remain/last) + همی (ever/always) = “the world does not remain (forever) for anyone.” The final بر کسی marks the experiencer/beneficiary: “for anyone.”
segments.s226-g342: نباید is the impersonal negative modal “one must not / it should not be.” بدو is the indirect object “to him/for him,” followed by the infinitival nominal phrase شاد بودن “to be glad.” بسی (an adverb/intensifier) qualifies the prohibition: “should not be excessively/much glad.” Put together: “one should not be overly glad for him.”
segments.s226-g343: همی is an adverb “always/ever.” مردمی here functions as an adjective or substantive meaning “popular / of the people” (often taken as ‘rule of the people’ or ‘public support’), and باید again expresses necessity “must/be necessary.” The conjunction و links to راستی, a noun meaning “truthfulness / uprightness.” The phrase asserts that popular rule and uprightness should always exist.
segments.s226-g344: ز (from) introduces a causal/source phrase: ز کژی (from crookedness/wrongness). بود is the existential/copular verb “there is / is,” followed by کمی و کاستی = “scarcity and deficiency / some lack.” The construction states that crookedness produces shortage and defect.
segments.s226-g345: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when'. پیران is the (plural) subject 'the elders'. بیامد is the past tense 3rd‑person 'came' (classical form with the prefixed ب‑). بر من دمان is a prepositional phrase: بر = 'toward/to', من = object pronoun 'me', دمان = 'skirt/hem' used figuratively ('to my side/at my skirts').
segments.s226-g346: سخن is the noun 'speech/words' and is the thing spoken. گفت is the past verb 'spoke/said' (classical usage). با introduces an instrumental/attitudinal phrase 'with', درد دل = 'pain of heart' i.e. 'sorrow/heartache', and یک زمان 'for a while / for a moment' marks duration.
segments.s226-g347: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/what'. از here means 'concerning' or 'with regard to' (i.e. 'about the good one'); نیکوی is used substantively 'the good/virtuous (one)'. با سیاوش = 'toward/with Siyâvash' (marks the person affected). چه کرد = interrogative 'what did (he) do' with کرد = past 'did/made' and an omitted subject understood from context.
segments.s226-g348: چه here is 'what' (interrogative). آمد is the past 'came/happened'. برویش is a compound meaning 'upon him' (بـ + رویش) so آمد برویش = 'befell him'. ز is the poetical form of 'از' meaning 'from/of', تیمار = 'care/solicitude', و = 'and', درد = 'sorrow/affliction'.
segments.s226-g349: فرنگیس و کیخسرو are two coordinated names ('Farangis and Kâi Khosrow'). از اژدها ('from the dragon') is a prepositional phrase marking separation/liberation — they are being freed from the dragon.
segments.s226-g350: بگفتار is a prefixal phrase 'by/through (his) words' (بـ + گفتار: instrumental). و links 'and', کردار = 'deeds/actions'. او is the possessive/pronoun 'his' (referring to the agent just spoken of). شد is past 'became', and رها is the predicate adjective 'free/unbound' (شد رها = 'became free').
segments.s226-g351: ابا is an archaic particle expressing reluctance/contradiction (here 'yet'/'although'). آنک = 'that/which' introduces the content clause. اندر دلم = 'in my heart' (locative). م is an archaic/enclitic copular marker (classical clitic related to 'is/there is') and here helps the existential construction. شد درست = 'became true/clear' (شد = 'became', درست = 'certain/correct').
segments.s226-g352: که again introduces the subordinate content 'that'. پیران = 'the elders' (subject). بکین is a poetic/archaic verb form meaning 'to take vengeance / to do (vengeful action)' (here instrumental idea: by vengeance). کشته is the past participle 'killed' and آید (literally 'comes') forms a periphrastic passive/result ('will be killed' / 'shall be slain'). نخست = 'first' (they will be the first victims).
segments.s226-g497: فریبرز is the subject (a proper name). بر is the preposition 'on/onto' governing the following noun. میسره here functions adjectivally/nominatively with جای (i.e. 'a convenient/attainable place'); جای is the place noun. جست is the past-tense verb 'leapt/sprung' (classical poetic past from جهیدن) and completes the clause 'X بر Y جای جست' = 'X leapt onto Y place'.
segments.s226-g498: دل نامداران is a nominal phrase 'the hearts of the renowned' (دل = 'heart'; نامداران = 'the famed/illustrious ones' used attributively). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. کینه is the object of ز ('hatred, grudge'). بشست is a past tense verb with the archaic prefix ب- meaning 'washed (away)'; together 'دل ... ز کینه بشست' = 'he washed the hearts (away) from hatred' (i.e. removed their hatred).
segments.s226-g499: بقلب اندرون is a locative phrase 'in/into the inner heart' (بقلب = 'in/into the heart', اندرون = 'inside, inner'). طوس is a proper name 'Tūs' that is linked as possessor/scene of the inner heart. نوذر plus the following بپای نماند form the verbal idea: نوذر بپای نماند = 'Nowzar did not abide/persist' (بپای here carries the sense 'to persist, to remain'). Word order places the locative + possessor before the person who fails to persist.
segments.s226-g500: نماند is the negative past 'did not remain/was not left'. آن is the demonstrative 'that'. زمان‌بر is an adjective 'time‑consuming/protracted' modifying زمین (here describing a span or situation). زمین نیز جای literally 'earth also place'—word order is poetic: the clause means 'that protracted (thing) on the earth likewise had no place / was not left' (i.e. 'there remained no place on earth for delay').
segments.s226-g501: تهمتن is the subject (epithet/name: 'Tahmatan' = 'the mighty one'). بیامد is the past verb 'came'. بپیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in front of the army' (بپیش = 'toward/in front of', سپاه = 'the host/army'). The clause is simple subject + past verb + locative phrase.
segments.s226-g502: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'. دارد is present/persistent sense 'has/keeps' (here used in the sense 'keeps/guards'). یلان را is the definite object 'the heroes' (را marks them as the object/topic of نگه داشتن). ز دشمن is the prepositional phrase 'from the enemy'. نگاه is a noun form related to نگه داشتن ('keeping/watch'), so together 'دارد یلان را ز دشمن نگاه' = 'he keeps/guards the heroes from the enemy' (i.e. 'who protects the heroes against the enemy').
segments.s226-g503: و زان روی begins the clause 'and for that reason / and on that account' (زان = 'from that/therefore', روی = 'face/side' used idiomatically 'on that account'). خاقان is the subject 'the khaghan' (ruler). بقلب اندرون is again the locative 'in the inner heart'. The verb is omitted/elliptical (a common poetic device), so the grouping means 'and for that reason the khaghan in his inner heart (was ... )'.
segments.s226-g504: ز پیلان زمین is a poetic genitive/measure phrase 'of/from the elephants of the earth' (پیلان = 'elephants' used literally or figuratively as 'giants/huge ones', زمین = 'land/earth'). چون که is the comparative/conjunctive 'as/like' and بیستون is a place-name (Bisotun) used as a simile. Together the fragment reads 'like the elephants of the earth, like Bisotun' — a simile describing great size/strength; note the clause lacks an explicit finite verb (ellipsis).
segments.s226-g369: Word order: determiner/pointer + noun phrase then verb. ورین (‘the foregoing/these’) points to the men just mentioned; نامداران is the noun 'renowned ones'. ابا is an archaic verb 'to refuse' referring back to those men (third-person plural, past implied). تخت و پیل are the direct objects 'throne and elephants'.
segments.s226-g370: This is a verbless descriptive clause: سپاهی 'the army/force' followed by the adverbial phrase بدین سان 'in this way' (an archaic compound), then a simile introduced by چو 'like/as' and دریای نیل 'the Nile'. The clause thus likens the army's manner to the Nile.
segments.s226-g371: فرستند is the classical past tense 'they sent' (3rd person plural). نزدیک ما is a locative/benefactive phrase 'near us / to our presence' and تاج و گنج are the direct objects 'a crown and treasure'.
segments.s226-g372: This small clause is idiomatic: ازایشان (از ایشان) functions as 'from them', نباشیم is 1st-person-plural negative copula/auxiliary 'we shall not be / we will not lack', and زین پس (zīn pas) is an adverbial 'henceforth/after this' modifying the statement; برنج is the noun 'rice' as the thing not to be lacking. (Some items are archaic compounds and the connectors are condensed in verse.)
segments.s226-g373: نداریم is the present/plural negative 'we do not have' (verb + personal ending). The noun phrase that follows functions as its object: گیتی 'the world' is the object of the infinitive بکشتن 'to kill/destroy', and نگاه 'the look/gaze' is the head noun modified by that infinitive (i.e. 'a gaze that kills the world').
segments.s226-g374: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/for'. نیکی‌دهش is a compound noun meaning 'the giving of good / the benefaction' (treated as the thing under discussion) and را is the object/topic marker. جز اینست راه is an inverted copular idiom: 'there is no way except this' (اینست = 'this is', راه = 'way/method'), so the clause says this is the only means of doing good.
segments.s226-g375: This is a nominal clause with the copular element integrated into the poetic compounds: جهان 'the world' followed by پر ز + noun + ست (contracted) yields 'is full of X' — here 'پر ز گنجست' = 'is full of treasure' and similarly 'پر تاج و تخت' = 'full of crowns and thrones.' The connective و links the two 'full of' phrases.
segments.s226-g376: نباید is an impersonal modal negative 'it should not / ought not', here introducing a general statement. همه 'all' is the subject, بهر یک is a prepositional phrase 'for one' (بهر = 'for/to'), and نیک‌بخت 'fortunate person' names the beneficiary. Together: 'It ought not to be that all [belong] to one fortunate person.'
segments.s226-g57: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' introducing the reason clause. تیره شب is a noun phrase 'dark night' (adjective before noun). و is the coordinating particle 'and' linking the phrase to the following predicate. دیده‌ام (literally 'my seen' / 'my sight') functions as the locus/possessor ('in my eye / in my sight'), and the verbless construction 'دیده‌ام نیست شرم' reads as 'there is no shame in my sight' (نیست = 'is not', شرم = 'shame').
segments.s226-g58: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause 'that / for'. من is the explicit subject 'I'. چند here means 'for some time / somewhat' (approximate extent). جوشیده‌ام is the perfect/resultative of جوشیدن with first‑person suffix, used metaphorically 'I have boiled / I have been seething' (emotional agitation). خون گرم is a noun + adjective 'hot blood' where گرم carries an emotional sense 'fervent / heated'. The clause as a whole explains the cause of there being 'no shame' in the previous segment.
segments.s226-g59: ز marks the object/source 'of' (poetic). کار سیاوش is a genitive construction 'Siavash's affair / matter'. چو here means 'when' (temporal). آگه is an adjective 'aware / informed' and شدم is the past (perfective) first‑person 'I became'—so 'چو آگه شدم' = 'when I became aware (of Siavash's matter)'. Word order follows classical Persian with verb at the end.
segments.s226-g60: ز ... و ز ... repeats the preposition 'by / from' for two coordinated sources: 'from the good and from the bad'. دست here means 'help / aid' (metaphorical 'hands' = assistance). کوته شدم is a compound in which کوته 'short' combines with the past verb شدم 'I became' to mean 'I became short-handed / was left without help'. The sequence therefore says he was left without assistance from either kind.
segments.s226-g61: میان is the preposition 'between'. دو کشور is 'two countries / two realms' (numeral + noun). دو شاه بلند repeats the pattern for 'two kings' with بلند 'lofty / high' after شاه; classical Persian often places adjectives after the noun. The whole phrase sets the spatial/political frame 'between two realms and two great kings.'
segments.s226-g62: چنین is an adverb/adjective 'thus / so' qualifying his state. خوارم is the adjective خوار 'despised' with the first‑person enclitic -م 'I am despised'. و connects predicates. زار is another predicate 'wretched'. و links again and دل مستمند is a nominal phrase 'heart needy' (مستمند 'needy, afflicted'); the copular sense is understood: 'I am thus despised and wretched and (my) heart is needy.'
segments.s226-g63: فرنگیس را marks the direct object with the accusative/topic marker را ('Ferangis'). من is the explicit subject 'I'. خریدم is the past tense 'I bought'. بجان is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/with the life (of)', an idiom meaning 'at the cost of my life' (i.e. I bought her with my life). Word order is subject–verb with a fronted object for emphasis.
segments.s226-g64: پدر is the subject 'her father'. بر سر آورده is a verbal phrase built from آورده (past participle of آوردن 'to bring') with the prepositional element بر سر (literally 'upon the head'); together this idiomatically can mean 'presented / brought forward / set upon (one's) head' or 'placed in a prominent position'. بودش is the past auxiliary بود with enclitic -ش which here stands for the third‑person object 'her' (classical Persian attaches the object clitic to the auxiliary), so آورده بودش = 'had brought her'. زمان 'in his time' / 'formerly' sets the temporal frame. Grammatically this is a past perfect style construction: 'her father had brought her (in his time).'
segments.s226-g17: بدو گفت is a short reporting phrase 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb); گفت is the past verb 'said' (here listed senses did not include the verb sense). من رستم زابلی is the quoted self-identification: the subject pronoun من precedes the name for emphasis, and زابلی is a postnominal nisba/adjective 'of Zābul' specifying the name.
segments.s226-g18: زره‌دار functions as an appositive noun 'armored man/armor-bearer' describing the speaker; با introduces an instrument or accompaniment 'with'; خنجر کابلی is a noun + adjectival nisba 'a dagger of Kabul' (خنجر = dagger, کابلی = Kabul‑born/Kabulite), with the adjective following the noun in typical Persian poetic order.
segments.s226-g19: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard'; پیران here is the proper name Pīrān (though the provided senses list only generic meanings), and ز پیش سپاه is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/in front of the army' (ز = 'from', پیش = 'front/before', سپاه = 'host/army').
segments.s226-g20: بیامد is the past 'came'; بر رستم uses بر in the sense 'toward/against' followed by the proper name رستم as the target; کینه‌خواه is a compound epithet (کینه 'grudge/hatred' + خواه 'seeking/desiring') functioning as a descriptive phrase 'seeking vengeance' applied to the subject (implicit Pīrān).
segments.s226-g21: بدو گفت رستم = 'Rostam said to him' (same structure as above). که‌ای پهلوان is an archaic/poetic vocative construction (که + ای) addressing the addressee: 'O champion!' with که here functioning as part of the address rather than a subordinate conjunction.
segments.s226-g22: درودت is 'your greeting(s)' (درود + enclitic -ت). ز introduces source 'from', خورشید is 'the sun', and روشن‌روان is a poetic compound adjective 'bright‑soul(ed)/bright‑souled' qualifying the sun; the whole is a poetic blessing: 'greetings to you from the sun, bright‑souled.'
segments.s226-g23: هم 'also' opens the addition. از مادرش literally 'from/of his mother' (ش is the possessive clitic 'his'), and دخت افراسیاب is an appositive identification 'the daughter of Afrasiab' specifying which mother is meant; the sequence means 'also (greet) his mother, the daughter of Afrasiab.'
segments.s226-g24: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying 'daughter' ('who/that'). مهر تو بیند is literally 'may she see your affection' — بیند here has the sense of present/subjunctive 'may see' or simple present 'sees' in a poetic jussive context. همیشه 'always' modifies the seeing, and بخواب is a poetic contraction of به خواب 'in sleep' (i.e. 'in her sleep/in dreams'), so the clause means 'may she always see your love in her sleep/dreams.'
segments.s226-g457: ز introduces the source/reason ('out of' or 'with') followed by the noun phrase نیکو گمان 'a good/favorable opinion'. اندر آیم places the verb (آیم, 1sg present/subjunctive of آمدن) after the prepositional phrase; نخست is an adverb 'first' modifying the whole clause. The sequence literally reads 'From a good opinion I come in first' → 'I will first adopt a favorable opinion.'
segments.s226-g458: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one should not / it must not be' (present). مگر here means 'except / unless' and introduces the limiting condition. جنگ و پیکار is a coordinated noun phrase 'war and combat/battle'. جست is used as the main verb 'to seek' (infinitival/lexical use after نباید) — together: 'one should not, unless (there is cause), seek war and combat.'
segments.s226-g459: چنو is a subordinating particle 'when/if/as'. باز گردد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive/future-like form (گردد = 'turns/returns') with باز 'back/again', so 'he turns back.' ز گفتار خویش is a prepositional phrase 'from his speech/words' (خوی + ش functions as 'his'), giving 'if/when he retracts his words.'
segments.s226-g460: ببیند is 3rd-person singular present/future 'he will see' (compound/doubling prefix بی- marks the stem دیدن). ز ما 'from/by us' indicates the agent/source of what he will perceive. درد و تیمار خویش: coordinated nouns 'pain and solicitude/care' followed by خویش ('his own'), so the phrase means 'he will see our care and his own suffering' or 'our concern for his pain.' Word order puts the verb first and the possessed noun phrase after the prepositional phrase.
segments.s226-g461: برو here functions as an exclamatory/imperative particle ('go!' or an exclamation like 'well done, go on') followed by آفرین 'bravo/praise' as the object of کرد ('made/said'). کرد is the past-tense verb 'they made/said' placed after the quoted/exclamatory material, a common classical order: 'They said “Boro, afrin”.' The proper names follow as the agents: گودرز و طوس.
segments.s226-g462: که introduces a reported/explanatory clause 'that'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'; بر تو is a prepositional phrase 'against/on you'; ندارد is the negative present 'has not'; فسوس is the object 'insidious whisper / malign whisper' — literally 'that the sun has no whisper against you', i.e. no ill-omen or treacherous whisper affecting you.
segments.s226-g463: بنزدیک تو is a locative/prepositional phrase 'near you' (بنزدیک = 'close at hand / near'); the following coordinated nouns بند و رنگ و دروغ list the things to be kept away: بند 'fetter/bond', رنگ 'appearance/guise' (here with the sense of deceptive appearance), و linking 'and', دروغ 'lie/falsehood'. The sequence is a noun list modifying the locative frame 'near you'.
segments.s226-g464: سخنهای پیران is a genitive phrase 'the words of the elders' (سخنهای = speeches/words, پیران = elders). نگیرد is a negative present/subjunctive/optative form of گرفتن 'to take/seize' (here 'does not take / will not seize'); فروغ is the object 'radiance/splendor'. Word order places the possessor phrase before the verb and the object after it: 'the elders' words do not seize (take away) radiance.'
segments.s226-g385: بگویم is a 1st‑person verb form expressing the speaker's intent ('let me tell' / 'I will tell'); it stands before its object, which is داستان 'a story'. یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain', and پیش تو is a prepositional phrase 'in your presence / to you' that follows the object for emphasis.
segments.s226-g386: کنون is an adverb 'now' placed before the verb. بشنو is a 2nd‑person imperative 'listen' (addressing the addressee). از گفته باستان is a prepositional phrase 'from / of the said/said‑thing of antiquity' where گفته functions as 'that which is said / the tale' and باستان qualifies it as 'ancient'.
segments.s226-g387: که is a clause‑linker 'that/for' introducing an explanatory clause. از راستی is a fronted prepositional phrase 'from truth', followed by جان بدگوهران 'the souls of the ill‑natured' (بدگوهران modifies جان), so the whole segment means 'that from truth the souls of the bad‑hearted (are) ...'.
segments.s226-g388: گریزد is a 3rd‑person present (or general) verb 'flees / escapes'. چو introduces a simile 'as / like', گردون is 'the sky / heavens', ز marks source 'from', and بار گران is the noun phrase 'heavy burden' (بار = load/burden, گران = heavy). Together: 'flees ... as the heavens from a heavy burden.'
segments.s226-g389: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle (here: 'if by chance / if ever'); بیچاره is an epithet 'wretched / unfortunate'. پیمان کند is the verb phrase 'makes a pact' (پیمان = covenant, کند = does/makes).
segments.s226-g390: بکوشد is a 3rd‑person verb 'he tries / strives'. که introduces the subordinate/purpose clause 'that / to', آن is a demonstrative 'that', راستی is 'truth / uprightness', and بشکند is the subjunctive/optative form 'break' (so: 'he strives to break that truth').
segments.s226-g391: چو here means 'when'. کژ is an adjective 'crooked / perverse' and آفریدش is the verb 'created him' (آفرید + -ش 'him'). جهان‌آفرین is the agent 'the world‑creator' (Creator of the world). The clause says 'when the Creator of the world made him crooked.'
segments.s226-g392: تو is the addressed pronoun 'you'. مشنو is a negative imperative 'do not listen / do not hear'. سخن زو means 'the speech/words of him' (زو = from him). و is the conjunction 'and'. کژی is the noun 'crookedness' and مبین here functions as a negative imperative 'do not see / do not perceive' (an archaic negative‑imperative form). The whole gives the exhortation: 'you, do not listen to his words and do not notice his crookedness.'
segments.s226-g185: بکوبند is a 3rd-person plural verbal form (present/imperative sphere in epic style) from کوب implying 'they strike/beat'; the object marker ما را follows to mark 'us' as definite object. بنعل ستور is a short postpositional phrase 'horseshoe/hoof of the steed' (بنعل = horseshoe; ستور = steed/horse) that functions as the instrument/agent of the verb (i.e. 'by/under the horses' hooves'). Word order is inverted for poetic effect (verb first).
segments.s226-g186: شود is the intransitive copular/auxiliary 'becomes' (3rd‑person singular). آب این بخت is a genitive/compound image 'the waters (or stream) of this fortune' (آب used figuratively as 'tide/flow'); بیدار modifies بخت ('this fortune awoken'), and شور is a predicate noun meaning 'tumult/commotion' — so the clause says the 'water/tide' of this awakened fortune 'becomes' turbulent.
segments.s226-g187: ز + هومان is a causal/relational prepositional phrase 'because of Homan' (ز in poetic register). دل من is the topic/head 'my heart'; بسوزد is a present/jussive form from سوختن used here in the sense of continual burning/aching ('keeps burning' or 'is consumed'), and همی is an adverb of continuative aspect 'always/continually'.
segments.s226-g188: ز introduces another cause/agent phrase ('from' or 'because of'); رویین روان is a compound noun phrase 'the iron‑souled one' (رویین = iron‑hard, روان = soul/spirit) acting as the subject, and برفروزد is a 3rd‑person singular verb meaning 'kindles/sets alight' (here figurative: arouses or increases passion/anger). همی again gives continuous aspect 'ever/continually.'
segments.s226-g189: دل رستم is a genitive construction 'Rostam's heart' (noun + name). آگنده is a predicative adjective meaning 'filled' and takes a following از phrase to mark content ('آگنده از کین' = 'filled with hatred'); او is the pronoun linked to کین ('hatred of him'), and ست is the archaic copula 'is' placed after the pronoun (poetic word order: predicate then copula).
segments.s226-g190: یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/utterly'; پر از چین is a prepositional phrase 'full of wrinkles' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of', چین = 'wrinkle'). اوست here again supplies the copula 'is' with the pronoun; the initial element بروهاش is a compact/poetic locative/relational expression tied to 'his appearance/face' (in context it marks the locus whose surface is full of folds). The clause as a whole says 'as for him, his face/appearance is wholly full of wrinkles.'
segments.s226-g191: پر از غم is an adjectival phrase 'full of grief' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of', غم = 'sorrow'). شوم is a 1st‑person present/jussive form 'I become / may I be' (expressing the speaker's condition); پیش خاقان چین is a locative/prepositional phrase 'before the Khagan of China' where پیش = 'in the presence of/before' and چین specifies the khagan.
segments.s226-g192: بگویم is the 1st‑person singular present/jussive of 'to say' ('shall I say / should I tell'); که introduces an indirect question/that‑clause; ما را uses را as the object marker on 'us'; چه آمد is an idiom 'what has come / what befell' (آمد here = 'happen' in the sense 'befall'); ز کین is a causal phrase 'from/because of enmity'. The whole clause asks whether the speaker should report what misfortune befell them because of this hatred.
segments.s226-g473: بایرانیان گفت کامشب بمی is a reporting clause: بایرانیان is the speaker 'the Iranians', گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used to introduce direct speech), and کامشب بمی is the quoted time‑phrase + verb phrase 'this night I will die/tonight I will...' (کامشب = 'this night'; بمی is an archaic optative/finite form meaning roughly 'I die' or 'let me die' in older Persian). Word order follows Subject – Verb – (quoted phrase).
segments.s226-g474: یکی اختری افگنم نیک‌پی is the content of the speech: یکی 'a certain/one', اختری 'a star' (poetic use), افگنم is an archaic first‑person verbal form of افکندن/افگن (optative/subjunctive) meaning 'I will cast/bring down', and نیک‌پی is a postpositive modifier 'well‑born / of noble lineage'. The noun phrase 'یکی اختری نیک‌پی' is the direct object of the verb افگنم.
segments.s226-g475: که فردا من این گرز سام سوار begins a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that'/'so that' or simply introducing content). فردا 'tomorrow' sets time, من 'I' is the subject, این 'this' modifies گرز 'mace/club', and سام سوار is a name with an epithet 'Sam, the rider' (سام is the proper name, سوار qualifies him as 'mounted/rider'). The sequence is time → subject → noun phrase (the mace belonging to Sam the rider).
segments.s226-g476: بگردن بر آرم کنم کارزار contains the verbal nucleus: بگردن بر is a prepositional phrase 'around/on the neck' placed before the verb; آرم is an archaic/poetic finite form used here with the sense 'I will place / I will wear' (poetic usage not identical to the common modern verb 'to rest'), کنَم is the 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used periphrastically 'I will do / I will make', and کارزار 'battle' is the object/complement. Together the phrase means 'I will put (it) about my neck and make battle.'
segments.s226-g477: از ایدر بران سان شوم سوی جنگ is composed of a sequence of adverbials plus the verb شوم: از ایدر 'from here', بران 'thence/therefrom' and سان 'thus/so' are adverbial modifiers that emphasize manner and origin, شوم is a 1st‑person optative/finite 'I shall go/become' in older usage, and سوی جنگ 'toward the battle' gives the direction. The overall meaning is 'from here I will thus go toward the battle.'
segments.s226-g478: بدانگه کجا پای دارد نهنگ is a short clause meaning 'thereupon / then, where the nahan(n)g has its foot'. بدانگه 'thereupon/at that moment' sets the temporal frame; کجا 'where' is a question/adverb modifying the following verb; پای 'foot/base' is the patient/locative noun and دارد is the present indicative 'has/stands (in a place)'; نهنگ is the subject 'the whale/monster' (here epic/figurative). Word order places the locative question‑word before the verb and the verb دارد governs the relation.
segments.s226-g479: سراپرده و افسر و گنج و تاج is a simple noun list joined by و 'and'. سراپرده 'inner curtain/behind the curtain', افسر 'helmet' (here an item of regalia), گنج 'treasure', and تاج 'crown' are enumerated without additional case‑marking; Persian commonly strings such nouns with و to form a coordinated list.
segments.s226-g480: همان ژنده پیلان و هم تخت عاج continues the list with contrast/emphasis: همان 'those very/that same', ژنده modifies پیلان as an adjective ('tattered / skins of elephants' — poetically 'elephant‑skins'), و هم 'and also', تخت 'throne' and عاج 'ivory' (adjective/noun 'ivory throne'). همان points back to the preceding items and هم adds 'also'.
segments.s226-g209: مگر here is a rhetorical/hortative particle meaning 'why not' or 'shall we not'. سیستان را is the definite object (را marks the direct object); پر آتش is an adjectival phrase 'full of fire' (پر = 'full (of)') placed before the noun آتش. کنيم is first-person plural present-subjunctive/hortative 'let us make/set', so the whole clause means 'let us set Sistan ablaze'.
segments.s226-g210: بریشان is a fused prepositional phrase (بر + ایشان) = 'upon them' or 'on them'. شب و روز is the standard 'night and day' coordinate. ناخوش کنيم uses ناخوش ('unhappy/unpleasant') as a predicative/adjectival complement and کنيم (1pl subjunctive/hortative) to mean 'let us make (them) unhappy'; object (them) is resumed by -شان.
segments.s226-g211: سر رستم زابلی را places the definite direct object (سرِ رستمِ زابلی) before the verb; را marks it. بدار is an imperative/optative form meaning 'hang' or 'to the gallows' (classical poetic usage), so the clause commands 'hang the head of Rostam of Zabul.'
segments.s226-g212: برآریم is first-person plural subjunctive/optative ('let us raise' / 'we will raise'). بر سوگ آن نامدار is a prepositional phrase 'upon the mourning for that famed one' (بر = 'on/over', سوگ = 'mourning', آن نامدار = 'that renowned [man]'). The verb thus applies to placing the head on the mourning/pyre for the famed man.
segments.s226-g213: تنش را places 'his body' as the definite object (تن + possessive -ش, then را). بسوزیم is 1pl subjunctive/hortative 'let us burn' (classical present). The conjunction و links to the following nominal phrase خاکسترش 'his ashes' which serves as the next object/topic.
segments.s226-g214: همی is a classical preverbal particle indicating continuous or emphatic action (here marking the verbal clause). برفشانیم is 1pl subjunctive 'let us scatter' or 'we will scatter'. گرد درش = 'around him/around it' (گرد = 'around/about', در + ش = 'in/at his'), so together 'we will scatter (his) ashes around him.'
segments.s226-g215: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. کین همی جوید is a verb phrase 'seeks vengeance' where همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle and جوید is 3rd person singular present/subjunctive 'seeks'. افراسیاب follows as the subject (poetic word order often places the subject after the verb).
segments.s226-g216: نه ... نه ... frames a correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. آرام باید که یابد reads idiomatically as 'he will find no peace'—باید here functions as a modal/impersonal auxiliary ('it is to be/there will be') and که introduces the subordinate verb یابد ('find'). خواب is the second coordinated noun 'sleep', so the clause states he will find neither calm (آرام) nor sleep (خواب).
segments.s226-g417: This is a nominal/adverbial phrase: کنون (now) sets the time; با + تو (with you) shows accompaniment/possession ('with you, O champion'); پهلوان is a vocative/title following تو‌ای. There is no finite verb here because the copula is deferred to the clause that follows. همی (not present here) is not used; word order is time — accompaniment — addressee — title — noun.
segments.s226-g418: یکی دیگر (one/another) is the agent/topic; افگند is a simple past/present classical form 'cast/threw' (here narrative: 'cast' or 'set in motion'); بازی (a game/contest) is the direct object; براه ('into the way/in motion') indicates the direction or commencement of the action. The clause therefore means 'another cast/put a game into motion.'
segments.s226-g419: جز از ... نداند همی is a negative-exception construction: جز از + nouns ('except from X and Y') + نداند (he does not know) with همی giving habitual/continuous sense. Thus 'he knows nothing except appearance and expedient.' Note classical order: exception phrase before the verb, and همی marks ongoing/habitual quality.
segments.s226-g420: ز دانش ('from/with respect to knowledge') is an ablative/source phrase modifying the verb برفشاند. برفشاند is an older/poetic present meaning 'he scatters/throws about'; with همی it expresses repeated/habitual action. سخن is the object ('words/speech'). Literally: 'from knowledge he scatters words' (i.e. he throws learned-sounding words about).
segments.s226-g421: کنون = “now.” از کمند تو is a prepositional phrase “by/from your snare” (از + noun + possessive تو). ترسیده شد combines the past participle ترسیده “frightened” with شد “became/was”; this is a stative/resultative construction meaning “he became frightened” or “he was rendered afraid.” Word order is typical: temporal adverb – agent/causal phrase – predicate.
segments.s226-g422: روا بد is an adjectival judgment: روا = “fitting/permissible” and بد = “bad/ill” — together here meaning “it was unfitting / unfortunate.” که introduces the subordinate clause “that … .” ترسیده (frightened) is repeated and followed by از دیده شد: دیده شد is the passive/perfect “was seen”; the preposition از before دیده here has a sense like “from sight / in being seen” (i.e., ‘he was seen’). The whole clause says it was unfortunate that, being frightened, he was seen (noticed).
segments.s226-g423: همه = “all / everyone.” پشت ایشان literally “at their back / behind them,” here meaning support or being at someone's rear. بکاموس is written as a single token (classical idiom: ب + proper name) and functions as a prepositional phrase meaning “with / at Kamus” or “backed by Kamus” (so: ‘Kamus was at their back’). بود is the past copula “was.” The overall sense: everyone had Kamus at their back / in support.
segments.s226-g424: سپهبد is the title “spahbed” (a high commander). چو is the simile particle “like / as.” سگسار is an adjectival or descriptive word meaning “mauled by dogs / dishonoured” (a strong poetic epithet). و links to the next clause; فر طوس = “the farr (divine/royal glory) of Tūs” (فر = farr / royal radiance; طوس = Tūs, the hero/name). بود again is the past copula “was.” Grammatically this gives two coordinated predications about the commander and the distribution of divine glory.
segments.s226-g65: بخانه = به خانه (to/in the house) placed before the rest for location; نهانش is نهان + clitic -ش ('her/it') meaning 'hidden (her)'. همی is the archaic continuative/emphatic particle ('used to/was'), and داشتم is the past imperfect 'I had/kept' (literally 'I was having/kept'). The whole segment reads literally 'At home, her-hidden I was keeping.'
segments.s226-g66: هرگز نه precedes the verb to give the negated frequency 'never'; برگاشتم here is a past first-person verb meaning 'I disclosed/revealed' (archaic/poetic form), so 'هرگز نه برگاشتم' = 'I never revealed (her)'. برو پشت is best read as adverbial/phrase-building pieces around the negation but does not alter the core negated verb (in speech the particle برو may be an intensifier/old connective); the sense in context is 'and I never disclosed her.'
segments.s226-g67: The phrase places the object/phrase بپاداش جان ('as a reward, the life') before the verb; خواهد is third-person future ('will want/demand'), از من marks the source/target ('from me'), and همی is the archaic emphatic/continuative particle. Literally: 'As recompense, life he will want from me.'
segments.s226-g68: سر here functions idiomatically as 'mind/inner thought' (part of the collocation meaning 'to be suspicious'); بدگمان is the adjective 'suspicious', خواهد is again future 'he will be', and از من ('of/from me') marks the object of suspicion. همی is the same archaic emphatic/continuative particle.
segments.s226-g69: پر از دردم = 'full of my pain' (پر predicate + از + درد + clitic-م 'my'). The vocative ای پهلوان is inserted addressing the hero. از دو روی means 'on/because of two sides' (روی = side/face). Word order is typical poetic inversion: predicate then its modifiers and a vocative.
segments.s226-g70: ز دو انجمن = 'from two assemblies/councils' (prepositional phrase). سر here means 'head/mind' and functions as the subject whose state is then described by پر از گفتگوی ('full of talk/conversation'). The line thus links external councils to the speaker's mind being full of discourse.
segments.s226-g71: نه negates the whole nominal-predicate that follows. راه گریزست is 'a way of escape' with گریزـست = گریز + است ('is escape/flight'), and ز افراسیاب indicates the source 'from Afrasiab'. The whole means 'There is no way of escape from Afrasiab.'
segments.s226-g72: نه جای دگر دارم places the negator before 'another place' and the verb دارم 'I have' follows, producing 'I have no other place.' آرام و خواب ('rest and sleep') are the kinds of repose being referred to and are governed by دارم; literal: 'I have no other place (for) rest and sleep.'
segments.s226-g505: This is a compact nominal string describing items placed on the right side: ابر ('cloud') is used poetically for a banner or ensign, followed by میمنه which specifies its position/auspiciousness ('the right/ right-hand'). کندر and شیر گیر stand in apposition: کندر (frankincense) is a noun, while شیرگیر is a compound where گیر ('to seize') forms an agentive sense attached to شیر ('lion'), i.e. 'lion‑catcher.' Persian often lists such nouns in sequence without a copula or conjunction.
segments.s226-g506: A noun phrase describing a person: سواری ('rider, horseman') is the head, followed by the epithet دلاور ('valiant/heroic') as a postpositive modifier. بشمشیر is the prepositional/ablative-like form 'ba-šamšīr' meaning 'with a sword' (literally 'by/with sword'), and و تیر simply adds 'and arrow(s)'. No finite verb appears; this phrase parallels the previous nominal listing.
segments.s226-g507: A prepositional/locative phrase: سوی ('toward') begins the phrase. میسره here functions adjectivally/attributively in the sense 'prepared/attainable' (poetic), modifying a compound nominal جنگ دیده گهار — جنگ + دیده ('war' + 'seen') together denote the battle/field, and گهار is a rare/obscure noun meaning 'place' or 'spot'. The whole reads as 'toward the prepared/appointed place of battle.'
segments.s226-g508: A short verbless clause describing the ground: زمین ('earth/ground') + خفته (past participle 'lying asleep') paints a poetic image of the earth being pressed/trampled. در زیر ('in/underneath') is the locative preposition governing نعل سوار ('the rider's horseshoe' or 'horseshoe of the mount'); the adjective/possessive relation is expressed by juxtaposition (نعل + سوار).
segments.s226-g509: همی گشت is a habitual/continuous past construction: همی (an old continuous marker, 'kept/was still') + گشت (past of گشتن, here 'went/advanced'). پیران ('the elders') is the subject, and به پیش سپاه ('to/before the front of the army') is the directional phrase (به + پیش + سپاه). The clause means 'the elders moved/advanced to the front of the host.'
segments.s226-g510: بیامد is the simple past 'came' (he/they came). بر is the preposition here meaning 'toward/upon/before' and governs the following name شنگل (an obscure or proper-name term). رزم‌خواه is an epithet 'war‑seeking' or 'warrior' modifying شنگل. The whole: 'they came up before Shangal, the war-seeker.'
segments.s226-g511: بدو here is a dative clitic-like phrase meaning 'to him' (i.e., 'said to him'); گفت is the past tense 'said' (the lemma entry given is conversational but the form functions as the verbal past of گفتن). کای is the vocative particle 'O', and نامبردار هِند is an epithet 'famed/renowned of India' addressing the addressee. The sequence is a direct speech introducer: 'he said to him: O famed one of India...'.
segments.s226-g512: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/against' introducing the opponent (ز بربر = 'against the barbarians' or 'from the barbarians'). بفرمان is the prepositional/agentive construction 'by the command' (ب- prefix + فرمان), تو is the second‑person pronoun 'your' (addressed to him). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / until', and بسند is an archaic subjunctive/optative plural form of 'بسیدن/بس' meaning 'to suffice / to be held/subdued' — here 'until they are rendered harmless/kept under control.'
segments.s226-g73: همم is a poetic noun meaning 'my aim/aspiration' (literally 'my effort'), placed before its nominal complement. The copula است follows the linked nouns (گنج و بوم) in classical Persian word order: '[My aim] treasure and homeland is'. The second و … هم coordinates an additional item (چارپای), with هم = 'also'. This whole chunk names what the speaker's aim/desire consists of.
segments.s226-g74: نبینم is a negative present-tense/optative form meaning 'I do not see' (lit. 'may I not see' in older usage but here plain negative). همی is an adverbial particle marking continuing/habitual aspect ('still/always'). روی رفتن بجای is a verbal-nominal phrase: رفتن = 'to go' is the verbal noun; بجای = 'in the place / instead'; روی here functions adverbially/nominally to mark the act of going (literary usage) so the clause means 'I do not see (that) going to the place is proper'. The clause therefore negates the propriety of leaving/going.
segments.s226-g75: پسر هست is an existential statement 'there are sons' (هست as copula/existential). و links the following noun پوشیده‌رویان (a plural agentive/adjectival noun 'those of veiled/modest faces'). بسی here is a classical adverb meaning 'many/much' (poetic usage). Word order is typical older Persian: [noun] هست و [group] بسی = 'there are X and many Y'.
segments.s226-g76: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' that modifies the following adjectives. خسته و بسته are two adjectives joined by و: خسته = 'tired/weary', بسته here means 'shut up/closed-off, withdrawn' (literal 'closed'). هر کسی literally 'each person' (everyone). The clause describes the condition of those just mentioned: 'each one is thus tired and withdrawn.'
segments.s226-g77: اگر introduces a conditional clause 'if'. جنگ is the object 'war/battle'. فرماید is third-person present/habitual from فرمودن used here in the sense 'he commands/orders' (literary third-person subjunctive/present). The name افراسیاب is the subject; word order is the normal literary conditional 'If Afrasiab orders war...'.
segments.s226-g78: نماند is negative present/future-like 'there will not remain / there will be no (time/occasion)'. که introduces a subordinate clause of result/content. چشم (the eye) + اندر آید (آید = 'comes') + بخواب (here a verbal noun/infinitival use 'to sleep' rather than an imperative) form the idiom 'the eye comes to sleep' = 'to fall asleep'. Together: 'there will be no time that the eye comes to sleep' → 'no time to sleep.'
segments.s226-g79: بناکام is an adjective 'unsuccessful/frustrated' modifying لشکر 'army/host'. بباید is an older/fixed form meaning 'one must' or 'it is necessary' (modal). کشید is the past/supine form of کشیدن (here meaning 'to draw/lead/withdraw' in a military sense). The phrase reads 'the (army) must be led/withdrawn in failure'—a terse classical expression where the verb follows the modal verb.
segments.s226-g80: نشاید is an impersonal present negative modal 'it is not fit/should not' (literary). ز indicates cause/reason 'because of / on account of'; فرمان او = 'his command'. آرمید here is an infinitival/personal form 'to rest' or perfective 'one rests' used after نشاید to express prohibition/unsuitability: 'it is not fitting to rest because of his command' (i.e., one must not rest at his order).
segments.s226-g145: شوم is an optative/performative particle here ('may I be' / 'let me'), used idiomatically with the following verb to introduce an utterance; بازگویم is 1st person singular present-subjunctive/precative 'I recount' or 'let me recount'; بگردان is an imperative/infinitival form 'render/turn' (here 'put/translate/turn into'), and همین is a demonstrative 'this very / the same' that selects the thing to be recounted. The sequence is a conventional narrator formula: 'Let me recount and render this (tale).'
segments.s226-g146: This short phrase names the addressee or manner. بمنشور is given as 'by charter/inscribed' in the lemma list but functions here as a prepositional/attributive piece indicating manner or a name; و is the coordinating 'and'; شنگل is an obscure/rare word (left untranslated in many editions) and is treated as a lexical name or technical term; بخاقان چین is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the Khāqān of China' (بِ + خاقان + چین), with چین here meaning 'China'.
segments.s226-g147: هیونی is a noun 'a beast/monster' (here 'savage creature'); فرستم is 1st person singular present/future 'I send / I will send'; بافراسیاب is a prepositional/compound phrase 'to/with Afrāsīāb' (i.e. directed at Afrāsīāb). The order is typical Persian: object/theme + verb + indirect specification.
segments.s226-g148: بگویم is the 1st person subjunctive/precative 'I will say' or 'let me say' (the lemma senses provided are nouns, so the correct verbal sense is not listed in the supplied senses); سرش را is 'his head' with ش as the possessive clitic and را marking the definite object; برآرم is 1st person subjunctive/present 'I lift/raise' (here idiomatically 'rouse'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; خواب is 'sleep' (here 'from sleep' = awake him). The clause expresses speech that will be reported: 'I will say “I will raise his head from sleep.”'
segments.s226-g149: و is the simple conjunction 'and'; زانجا is a compound 'from there' (زان + جا); بیامد is the past tense 'came'; بلشکر is a compound meaning 'with an army' or 'an army' (often used adverbially 'in force'); چو is the simile particle 'like/as'; باد is 'wind'. The word order is typical: place/time → verb → circumstance → simile.
segments.s226-g150: کسی here means 'someone' or 'some (men)'; را is the topical/definite object marker used to highlight 'کسی'; که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; بودند is the past plural of 'to be' ('they were' / 'were'); ویسه is the proper name 'Viseh'; نژاد means 'race/lineage'. Together the phrase reads as a relative specification: 'someone(s) who were of Viseh's race/lineage.'
segments.s226-g151: یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain (man)'; انجمن 'meeting/assembly' together with کرد 'did/made' forms the verbal phrase 'held a council' (انجمن کرد = 'he convened'); و is 'and'; بگشاد is here used as a verb 'opened/revealed' (the supplied senses list gives adjectival senses but in context it is the verbal past/preterite form 'opened'); راز is 'secret'. The whole segment means 'a man called a council and opened/revealed the secret.'
segments.s226-g152: چنین 'thus/so' sets up reported speech; گفت is the verb 'said' (the supplied lemma senses list gave noun senses for گفت, so the speaker sense is not listed and would be -1 here); کامد is an archaic/presentive form 'comes' or 'will come'; نشیب و فراز is a fixed pair meaning 'descent and ascent' or 'falls and rises' (i.e. 'ups and downs'). Grammatically this is reported speech: 'Thus he said: "the ups and downs will come."'
segments.s226-g121: This noun phrase strings together a guilty party and the object of guilt: گنهکار (the guilty one/sinner) + خون (blood/bloodshed) + سر بیگناه (the innocent's blood). The construction implies possession/affectedness: “guilty of the blood of the innocent.” The word-order is poetic/genitive: the thing (blood) and its qualifier (بیگناه) follow the guilty agent.
segments.s226-g122: سزدگر here functions adjectivally/participially meaning 'fitting/appropriate'; نباشد is the classical negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'is not' / 'should not be.' بدین is a prepositional form meaning 'in/at this' (ب + این) and رزمگاه is the location 'battlefield.' The clause literally: 'is not fitting in this battlefield.'
segments.s226-g123: و دیگر که introduces an additional hypothetical/conditional point ('and furthermore/if'). با من ببندی کمر is a causative/reciprocal-looking imperative/subjunctive clause: ببندی (present/subjunctive of بستن here 'gird/tie') takes کمر ('belt/waist') as its object and با من 'with me' as accompaniment — 'if you gird your belt with me.' Word order puts the prepositional accompaniment before the verb phrase, typical in Persian.
segments.s226-g124: بیایی is a hortative/subjunctive/imprecatory form meaning 'come' (an exhortation or conditional: 'if you come'). بر شاه uses بر in the sense 'against/upon' and شاه پیروزگر is the compound noun 'the victorious king' (پیروزگر modifying شاه). The clause completes the conditional: 'and come against the victorious king.'
segments.s226-g125: ز چیزی که ایدر بمانی همی is a conditional phrase: ز چیزی 'because/of something' or 'if there is something by which,' که introduces the relative clause, ایدر (classical form of 'here') + بمانی 'you would remain' and همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle attached to the verb. Together: 'if there is something by which you would remain here.'
segments.s226-g126: تو آن را گرانمایه دانی همی is the main clause: تو 'you' (subject) + آن را (that + obj. marker) + گرانمایه 'precious/valuable' + دانی ('you consider/deem' — present tense) with همی again as an emphatic/continuative particle. The literal reading: 'you deem that thing precious.'
segments.s226-g127: بجای یکی ده بیابی ز شاه is a compact clause: بجای 'in place of' + یکی 'one' sets the expectation, ده ('ten', omitted in the provided word list) + بیابی is the verb 'you will find/obtain' (future/optative sense in classical style), and ز شاه 'from the king' is the source. Word order places the quantity and verb together: 'you will find ten instead of one from the king.'
segments.s226-g128: مکن یاد بنگاه توران سپاه is an imperative prohibition: مکن 'do not' + یاد here means 'mention/recall' (i.e., 'do not bring to mind') and بنگاه functions as 'place/encampment' (here the locus of the enemy), followed by توران (Turan) and سپاه 'army/host.' The sentence: 'do not mention/recollect the Turanian army's camp.'
segments.s226-g313: بباید is a modal ('it is necessary / one must') that governs the verbal infinitive شدن here; شدن functions roughly as 'to come to be / to allow to pass' so the whole phrase means 'there must be a time / one must allow a time'. یک زمان is the duration 'a moment / a while'. زین میان (poetic order) locates that time 'in the midst / between these', though the word זین in the provided senses does not reflect the usual 'ز این' = 'from this'.
segments.s226-g314: نگه کرد is the compound verb نگه داشتن ('to keep/observe') split as noun نگه + auxiliary کرد; the phrase is inverted from the usual بايد نگه كرد and means 'one must keep/watch/consider'. بايد is the modal 'must'. بسود و زیان is a prepositional/comparative phrase 'with regard to advantage and loss' (i.e. weigh pros and cons).
segments.s226-g315: ببینی is 2nd person singular present-subjunctive form used here with a predictive sense 'you will see / you shall see'. کزین is a contracted classical preposition 'from this / of this' and introduces the source. لشکر بی‌کران is a noun + adjective 'a boundless army/host' (adjective follows the noun in Persian).
segments.s226-g316: جهانگیر is used as an epithet/adjective 'world-conquering' applied to the force just mentioned. The conjunction و links this epithet to the following prepositional phrase با گرزهای گران 'with heavy maces'; گرزهای is the plural formed with -های and گران is the adjective 'heavy/weighty' modifying it.
segments.s226-g317: دو بهره is a numeral + noun 'two shares/items/portions'. بود is the past copula 'there was / there were'. زیر خاک اندرون is a locative phrase 'under the earth, inside' (poetic word order places the location after the verb).
segments.s226-g318: This is a list of objects: کفن 'shroud' and جوشن 'armor/cuīrass' joined by و; ترگ 'rag' is qualified by the participle شسته 'washed', and بخون is an archaic/poetic 'با + خون' = 'with blood' (i.e. 'blood‑washed'). The participle agreement and the prepositional particle are typical of classical Persian noun modification.
segments.s226-g319: بدو is the old prepositional phrase 'to him / unto him'. گفت is the past verb 'he said' (the lemma senses listed do not show the verbal meaning, so mark as not matching the provided senses). کلباد‌ای تیغ‌زن is a vocative apposition: 'Kalbad, O swordsman' (name + epithet).
segments.s226-g320: چنین is 'thus/so'. تا here means 'as long as / while' and combines with توان 'you can / are able' to form 'as long as you are able'. فال بد را is the object 'the bad/ill omen' with را marking definiteness. مزن is the (negative) imperative 'do not strike / do not smite' (in the sense 'refrain from striking').
segments.s226-g545: This coordinate prepositional phrase uses two prepositions in parallel: the poetic preposition 'ز' and the common 'از', both meaning 'from/through'. 'ز جوش سواران' = 'from the surge/pressure of the horsemen' (جوش here is an abstract noun 'surge, bursting forth') and 'از دار و گیر' pairs two nouns ('دار' and 'گیر') joined with 'و'. The whole phrase functions adverbially, marking source or cause for what follows.
segments.s226-g546: This clause reads as a nominal/predicative construction: 'هوا دام' where 'هوا' (the air/atmosphere) is followed by the noun 'دام' (trap), i.e. 'the air became/was (like) a trap.' The next words 'کرگس بد از پر تیر' qualify the victim and cause: کرگس (the vulture) is described as being ill/affected ('بد') by ('از') the arrows' feathers (پر تیر). The sequence is compressed and poetic (agent/result relation is condensed rather than a full finite verb clause).
segments.s226-g547: 'کسی را نماند' is a negative existential: 'no one remained/was left.' The object marker 'را' after 'کسی' emphasizes the person affected. 'اندر آن دشت هوش' is a locative phrase ('in that plain') followed by the noun 'هوش' (awareness, sense) which is the thing that was not left — altogether: 'No one in that plain retained his wits/awareness.'
segments.s226-g548: This short clause gives the cause: 'ز بانگ تبیره شده کره گوش'. 'ز' = 'from/because of'; 'بانگ' = 'the cry/sound' is the source. 'تبیره شده' is a compound participial/periphrastic usage (شده = become/made) modifying or qualifying the cry, and 'کره گوش' is an archaic collocation referring to the ears (here meaning 'the ears were made [so/as they are]'). Grammatically the phrase explains why no one retained his senses: the cry (so described) affected the ears.
segments.s226-g549: 'همی گشت' is the frequentative/continuous past form meaning 'kept going/was roaming'. It precedes the subject 'شنگل' (an obscure/poetic name or term). 'میان دو صف' is a prepositional phrase 'between the two ranks/lines' indicating location. Word order is typical of Persian poetry: verb/adverbial element followed by the noun and its locative phrase.
segments.s226-g550: 'یکی تیغ هندی گرفته بکف' is a compact nominal phrase: 'یکی' = 'one/ a certain man'; 'تیغ هندی' = 'an Indian (style) sword' (modifier-noun order); 'گرفته' is a perfect participle meaning 'having taken' or 'holding'; 'بکف' (poetic) = 'in the hand'. Together: 'one (man) holding an Indian sword in his hand.' The participle phrase describes the man.
segments.s226-g551: 'یکی' again introduces 'a certain one'; 'چتر هندی' = 'an Indian-style canopy/umbrella' (modifier + noun). The sequence 'بسر بر' is an archaic/permuted order corresponding to Modern Persian 'بر سر' = 'on the head' (literally 'on head'). 'بپای' is a verbal/adjectival infinitive/participle meaning 'laying in wait' or 'abiding' (poetic). The whole segment describes a person who, with a canopy on his head, kept watch or lay in ambush.
segments.s226-g552: This is a terse enumerative phrase. 'بسی مردم' = 'many people' (بسی used poetically for 'many' or 'much'); 'از دنبر و مرغ و مای' lists sources or parties: 'از' = 'from'; 'دنبر' = 'the rear/flank', 'مرغ' = 'bird(s)', and 'مای' is an old form meaning either 'wine' or 'we/us' depending on context. Grammatically it's a coordination after the previous clause, giving additional participants or origins; the exact referents are archaic and compressed in the poetic line.
segments.s226-g441: سپه را = 'the host/army' + object marker را; object precedes the verb in Persian. بدان is a prepositional phrase here ('بِ' = to/with + an old/pronoun-like element) meaning 'to/with that', introducing the stratagem, and نواخت is a past verb used idiomatically 'to set/apply' (literally 'struck/played') so the whole clause reads 'he set/put the army to that device/stratagem.' Word order: object (سپه را) + prepositional phrase (بدان چاره) + verb (نواخت).
segments.s226-g442: ز گودرزیان = 'from/of the Gudarzis' (ز = from); گورستانی بساخت is a compact past clause meaning 'he made (them) a cemetery' — i.e. he caused many of the Gudarzis to be laid in graves. The verb phrase is elliptical/poetic: گورستانی (cemetery) is the effect/result and بساخت indicates the making/bringing-about.
segments.s226-g443: که تا زنده‌ام = a subordinating clause 'that/for as long as I am alive' (تا = 'until/so long as', زنده‌ام = 'I am alive' with enclitic ام = 'I/my'). خون سرشک منست is a compact predication: خون 'blood' + سرشک 'a small drop/teardrop' — 'my blood is but a drop'; منست is an archaic/poetic copular/predicative ending here tying the predicate to the speaker.
segments.s226-g444: یکی تیغ هندی پزشک منست lists possessions or resources: یکی 'one (thing)' introduces the first item 'an Indian sword' (تیغ هندی), and پزشک 'a physician' is given as another resource; the final منست again marks the predicative relation (poetic). The clause functions like 'one is an Indian sword, (another) a physician (I have).' Word order is nominal list + predicate.
segments.s226-g445: چو بشنید = temporal clause 'when he heard' (چو = 'when'); رستم is the subject and بگودرز گفت is the speech-act construction 'he said to Goudarz' (بـ- + name = 'to Goudarz', گفت = said). The clause orders the time clause first, then the subject and the indirect object and verb.
segments.s226-g446: که گفتار تو با خرد باد جفت is a blessing/wish clause introduced by که 'that'; گفتار تو 'your speech' is the theme, با خرد 'with wisdom' is a prepositional complement, and باد is the optative particle 'may' (a conventional wish), with جفت functioning predicatively 'paired/joined' — literally 'that your speech may be paired with wisdom.'
segments.s226-g447: چنین است پیران = 'thus are the elders' (چنین است, 'such is/are'); the inversion places the predicate before the noun for emphasis. و این راز نیست = 'and this is not a secret' (راز = secret, نیست = 'is not'). The sentence gives a general statement about elders and then asserts the obviousness of the point.
segments.s226-g448: که او نیز با ما هم‌آواز نیست = a subordinate clause 'that he too is not in accord with us': که introduces the clause, او نیز 'he also', با ما 'with us' (prepositional), هم‌آواز 'in unison/like-minded' functions as predicate adjective, and نیست 'is not' negates it. Overall: 'that he too does not agree with us.'
segments.s226-g529: This noun phrase describes the neck's ornament. گردن is the head noun 'neck'. از introduces the material/source relationship ('of' or 'made of'), linking گردن to طوق زر 'a collar of gold'. بیاراسته functions as a participial/adjectival element modifying گردن; in classical order the modifier precedes the head in poetry or is fused as a compound (here it qualifies the neck).
segments.s226-g530: میان بند کرده is a compound describing a girding at the middle: میان (middle) combines with بند (a band/bond) and the past participle کرده ('made'/'having bound') to mean 'girded about the middle'. بزرین is an attributive adjective 'great' placed before the noun کمر (waist/girdle) in a poetic/archaic word order to yield 'a great waist‑band' or 'a great girdle'.
segments.s226-g531: فروهشته is a perfect/passive participle meaning 'scattered' or 'spread out' and governs the following origin phrase. از marks origin ('from the elephant'), so the whole phrase reads 'brocade folds from the elephant were spread'. دیبای چین is a genitival noun phrase 'folds (چین) of brocade (دیبا)', with دیبای qualifying چین.
segments.s226-g532: نهاده is a past participle meaning 'placed/put'. برو تخت here is an archaic/poetic locative: برو is a contracted form corresponding to بر (on/upon) plus the clitic, so برو تخت = 'upon the throne'. The conjunction و links this clause to مهدی زرین; زرین is the adjective 'golden' following the noun مهدی in normal Persian noun–adjective order. (Note: مهدی here functions as an object/place noun in the line, not necessarily the male name; the listed senses do not include this meaning, so it is left unspecified.)
segments.s226-g533: برآمد is the past verb 'issued forth/rose'. دم is used here as 'blast' or 'breath' (the sound produced), and ناله qualifies that blast as 'a lament/woeful sound'. کرنای is in genitive relation (the trumpet that utters the lament): the phrase is an iḍāfa-like sequence 'the blast (دم) of the lament of the trumpet' and functions as the subject of برآمد.
segments.s226-g534: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past tense 'they went/advanced' (here better understood as 'they advanced'). پیلان جنگی is the direct noun phrase 'war elephants' placed immediately after the verb in poetic word order; ز جای is a simple prepositional phrase 'from their place(s)' marking departure. The sequence thus means 'the war elephants advanced from their places.'
segments.s226-g535: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. سوی is the preposition 'toward' and governs the following directional noun (here printed میسره, which in context functions as a directional/locative 'to the right/fortunate side'); the numeral phrase سی هزار follows to indicate quantity: 'thirty thousand came toward X.' The listed sense choices for میسره do not match its locative/directional use here, so its sense is unspecified.
segments.s226-g536: سواران is the noun 'horsemen/riders'. گردنکش and نیزه‌دار are postpositive adjectival descriptors joined by و: گردنکش 'headstrong/rebellious' modifies the riders, and نیزه‌دار 'spear‑bearing' specifies their armament. This is typical Persian noun + adjective(s) coordination: noun first, then one or more modifiers.
segments.s226-g481: This sequence gives two first-person singular verbs in the present/subjunctive form with -م (بیارم, سپارم) functioning narratively as 'I will/let me bring' and 'I will/let me entrust.' Word order is verb–verb–indirect object (the beneficiary بایرانیان), a compact poetic phrasing where the object/beneficiary follows the verbs.
segments.s226-g482: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). تاختن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'charging/attack' used as the object marked by را (definite object marker). ببندم is 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I close/block/stop,' and میان is an adverb/preposition 'in the middle / between,' completing the conditional clause 'if I close/stop the charge in the midst.'
segments.s226-g483: برآمد is a past/factual verb 'arose/issued forth' and خروشی is the noun 'a roar/clamor' that is presented as what arose. ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from the place/from their seats'; نشست here functions nominally as 'seat/assembly' (a noun meaning where they were sitting), so the whole unit means 'a clamor arose from the place of sitting.'
segments.s226-g484: ازان is a prepositional/pronominal form 'of/from those,' naming the source; نامداران is an adjective/noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones' modifying the implied people; خسروپرست is a compound noun 'Khosrow-devotees' (courtly sycophants). Together they specify whose seats produced the cry.
segments.s226-g485: سوی introduces direction 'toward'; خیمه خویش is 'their own tent(s)' (خویش = 'own' with the enclitic ش marking possession), and رفتند is the past plural 'they went/returned.' باز placed after the verb functions adverbially as 'back/again,' so the phrase means 'they returned to their tents.'
segments.s226-g486: بخواب here is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to sleep' (or 'sleep' as a nominalized verb), و is 'and,' بآسایش is an adverbial phrase 'in comfort/peacefully' (ب- prefix + آسایش), آمد is past 'came,' and نیاز is the noun 'supplication/need.' The clause states that sleep and peaceful repose came and a petition arose (i.e., they rested and offered a prayer/plea).
segments.s226-g487: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; خورشید is the subject 'the sun'; بنمود is the preterite of نمودن 'showed/displayed'; رخشان is an adjective 'radiant' placed before the noun as is common in poetry; and کلاه is 'helmet/cap' (a metaphorical or martial image). Together: 'When the sun displayed its radiant helmet.'
segments.s226-g488: The second چو again means 'when.' سیمین سپر (silvery shield) is the object described; دید is past 'saw/beheld' and takes رخسار as its object, with ماه 'the moon' as the possessor of that visage. Poetic syntax allows the image that the moon sees its own face (رخسار ماه) reflected in the silver shield.
segments.s226-g289: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' placed before the noun; پیل‌بازی is the noun 'elephant-display'. نمایم is the first-person present/optative form of نمودن used here to express the narrator's intention ('I will make/show'); بدوی is an adverbial qualifier ('in the old/primitive manner') placed after the verb phrase, a typical poetic order.
segments.s226-g290: کزان is a relative/prepositional compound 'from which' introducing the consequence; پس is temporal 'after/after that'; نیارد is a contracted/archaic verbal form (here functioning as a negative/future/preterite sense 'will not bring/turn'), a poetic variant of نیاورد/نیاورد which governs the following directional phrase; سوی رزم روی literally 'turn his face toward the battle'—سوی + noun shows direction and روی 'face' is the thing that turns.
segments.s226-g291: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past tense (third person) 'heard' with لشکر (the army) as the agent. ز + شنگل سخن is an ablative/genitive-type phrase 'speech from/of Shengel' (z marking source or provenance in poetic Persian).
segments.s226-g292: جوان شد is a copular/result clause 'became young' with شد as the past of شدن. دل is the grammatical subject ('the heart'); مرد گشته کهن follows and functions as a noun phrase modifying دل: مرد 'man' modified by گشته (perfect participle 'having become') and کهن 'old', so 'the heart of the man who had grown old'. The line uses flexible epic word order (predicate before its long subject modifier).
segments.s226-g293: بدو is the dative/clitic-like phrase 'to him'; گفت here is the past tense 'said' (note: in the supplied sense list 'گفت' was given as a noun meaning 'conversation', so the spoken-verb sense is not in that list and is marked below as -1). پیران is the subject 'the elders'. کانوشه بدی is the reported content/command: کانوشه (the casket) + بدی (here an archaic second-person past/imperative-form of 'to give') — in epic Persian the same form can be homographic with the noun 'badī', hence the clause-internal ambiguity.
segments.s226-g294: روان را uses روان 'soul' with the object marker را to mark the direct object. بپیگار is a prepositional/adverbial phrase indicating purpose or arena (literally 'for the fight/contest' — in epic usage it expresses 'for the struggle' or 'on the occasion of the contest'). توشه بدی is 'give a toshah/provision' — توشه 'provision' + بدی again the archaic 'give' form. Overall this is an imperative/report of an order: 'give the soul a provision for the battle/journey.'
segments.s226-g295: همه نامداران is a coordinated noun phrase 'all the nobles/notables'; و links this to خاقان چین where چین (here the country name 'China') is in postposition to خاقان, yielding 'the Khagan of China'. This is ordinary epic coordination and apposition: 'all the notables and the Chinese Khagan.'
segments.s226-g296: گرفتند is the simple past, third-person plural 'they took up' or 'they raised' (here 'they took up cries'); بر introduces the beneficiary/target 'for' or 'to' and is followed by شاه هند in genitive order 'the King of India' (شاه + هند). آفرین is a noun 'acclamation/cry of praise' (they 'took up acclamation' for the Indian king), used here as the object of گرفتند.
segments.s226-g249: یکی before the noun gives an indefinite reading 'a certain / a (single)'; the clause is Subject (یکی آتش) + Verb (آمد). ز چرخ کبود is a postposed prepositional phrase 'from the azure sky' where چرخ کبود is a fixed epithet ('the blue/azure heaven').
segments.s226-g250: دل ما شد is a simple predicative sequence 'our heart became'; شد is the perfective/become verb. از تف او is the causal/ablative phrase 'from his spit' and پر ز دود is a predicative phrase 'full of smoke' (پر used as adjective 'full (of)'); the whole clause means the heart was filled with smoke caused by his spit.
segments.s226-g251: کنون = 'now'. سر بسر is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'bit by bit' or 'throughout/entirely' and here adverbially modifies the following noun phrase. تیزهش 'تیزه' + enclitic -ش = 'its point/tip' and بخردان is a dative phrase 'to the wise' (ب- + خردان = 'to/for the wise'); this whole noun phrase functions as the object/indirect object of the following verb بخوانید.
segments.s226-g252: بخوانید is an imperative (2nd plural) here meaning 'call/summon' or 'read/recite' depending on context; followed by با + noun phrase 'with the mobeds and the rēdān', i.e. 'together with the priests and the rēdān'. با marks accompaniment. Note word order: the verb governs the previously given object phrase.
segments.s226-g253: ببینید is an imperative plural 'see / find out'. تا here introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that') linking to the subordinate clause چاره کار چیست ('what the remedy/solution of the affair is'). چی (interrogative) + ست (copula) form the question 'what is...'.
segments.s226-g254: بدین is a fused prepositional expression meaning 'in/at this' (به + این) and heads the locative phrase 'in this battlefield' (بدین رزمگه). مرد پیکار is a genitive/attributive phrase 'man of battle' (the warrior); کیست is the interrogative copula 'who is (it)'. Word order places the locative first, then the noun phrase, and the question-predicate ki-st at the end.
segments.s226-g255: همی رای 'this (very) counsel' or 'such counsel' (همی as an intensifier/adverbial particle). باید is the modal 'must/ought', and the clause که گردد درست is a subordinate clause introduced by که giving the desired result/purpose: گردد is a present/subjunctive-like 'become' and درست 'correct/right'.
segments.s226-g256: از آغاز 'from the beginning' (از = from; آغاز = beginning). کینه 'hatred, grudge' is the object, and نبایست is a negated modal 'ought not / must not'; جست here means 'to seek' (not the literal leap). The clause therefore orders against seeking enmity from the outset.
segments.s226-g393: نخستین که introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'when first' or 'the first time that'. ما is the 1st-person plural subject; رزمگه is the direct object 'battlefield/arena'; ساختیم is a past simple 1st-plural verb 'we built/made' placed at the end as is normal in Persian.
segments.s226-g394: سخن رفت is an impersonal/periphrastic phrase meaning 'word/talk went (around)' — رفت is used in the sense of 'go/ spread' rather than a physical leaving; زین کار is a prepositional phrase 'about/from this deed' (here the exact lexical sense 'زین' as 'of this' is not among the provided senses); و links the next clause; پرداختیم is 1st-plural past of پرداختن, here best rendered 'we set off / proceeded / moved on' (a poetic usage).
segments.s226-g395: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'; پیران is the plural noun 'the elders'; فرستاده functions as a noun 'a messenger' (literally 'one sent'); آمد is the past tense 'came'; برین is a locative/directional compound (بر + این) meaning 'to here / to this place'.
segments.s226-g396: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'who said'); بیزارم is an adjectival predicate with 1st-singular ending meaning 'I am weary/averse (to/of)'; از marks the source/target of that weariness; دشت is 'plain/field'; وز here is poetically written for وِ ز (i.e. 'and/of'), a fused particle not represented by the provided senses; رنج و کین are a coordinated pair 'toil/suffering and hatred/revenge.'
segments.s226-g397: که again introduces the content clause; من is the 1st-person pronoun; دیده is the noun 'eye(s)'; دارم is the present/factual 'I have' (1st-singular); همیشه 'always' modifies the clause; پر آب is a two-word adjective phrase meaning 'full of water' (i.e. tear-filled).
segments.s226-g398: ز here is the causal/prepositional 'from/because of' introducing the cause of the tears; گفتار و کردار are two coordinated nouns 'speech/words and deed/behavior'; افراسیاب is the proper name (the agent causing those words and deeds). The whole phrase supplies the cause for 'eyes full of water.'
segments.s226-g399: میان بسته‌ام is an idiom: میان بستن / میان بستنِ چیزی means to 'shut off' or 'close/terminate the tie' (here perfective 1st-singular 'I have closed/bound/ended'); بندگی شاه را is the object marked by را: 'the servitude/devotion to the king.' The auxiliary ending -ام marks the speaker as subject.
segments.s226-g400: نخواهم is the negative present/future-form of خواستن 'I will not want/I do not wish (to have)'; what is being refused is the coordinated list marked by را: بر و بوم و خرگاه — a conventional triad meaning 'land and home and stable/lowly shelter'; the repeated و is simple conjunction. The phrase is an object clause: 'I will have none of the land, the home, nor the stable.'
segments.s226-g561: Pronoun 'تو' (you/thou) is fronted for address. 'امروز' is a temporal adverb ('today') placed before the locative phrase. 'پیش صف' = 'in front of / before the battle line' (پیش = in front of; صف = battle line). 'اندر' is a locative preposition 'in/within'. 'مپای' is an archaic/poetic word here used as a predicate meaning to hang back / linger in place; its position at the end of the segment is typical of older Persian word order where the predicative element follows the locative phrase.
segments.s226-g562: 'مکن' is a prohibitive (do not) placed before the verb's object phrase; in classical style the negative imperative often appears after temporal phrases. 'امروز و فردا' ('today and tomorrow') here functions as the timeframe being forbidden — the idiom means 'do not put off to tomorrow.' 'رزم رای' is a genitive/compound noun meaning 'counsel/plan of battle' (رای = counsel, رزم = battle) and is the semantic object that the speaker orders not to postpone.
segments.s226-g563: 'پس' = 'then/therefore' opens this consequential clause. 'پشت' here means 'behind' (location). 'خاقان چینی' = 'the Chinese khagan' (word order modifier after noun is common in classical Persian: خاقان modified by چینی). 'بایست' is an older modal/imperative form meaning 'ought to stand' / 'stand (you should)'; here it gives a directive or recommendation.
segments.s226-g564: 'که' introduces the result/purpose clause ('so that / that'). 'داند' is 3rd-person present ('he knows'). The clitic 'ت' (= 'you') is the object attached to the verb and 'را' marks that object as definite/emphatic: 'he knows you.' 'با سواری دویست' is a prepositional phrase 'with two hundred horsemen' (با = with; سواری = horsemen/riders) modifying what he will know. Overall: 'stand behind the khagan so that he may know you are with two hundred riders.'
segments.s226-g565: The conditional particle 'که‌گر' (= اگر / if) introduces a protasis. 'زابلی' means 'a man from Zabul' (an agent/noun). 'با' is the preposition 'with' and 'درفش سیاه' = 'the black banner/standard' (درفش = banner, سیاه = black) describes that man. Word order places the conditional introducer first, then the subject with its accompanying phrase.
segments.s226-g566: 'ببیند' is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of 'دیدن' used here in the conditional clause ('sees' / 'should see'). The clitic 'ت' is the object 'you' and 'را' marks it as definite. 'کار' ('affair/affairs') is the subject of the following predicate. 'گردد' is an impersonal/predictive form of 'شدن' meaning 'becomes' or 'turns out', and 'تباه' is the predicate adjective 'ruined'. The sequence thus means 'if a Zābeli sees you with the black banner, the affair will become ruined.'
segments.s226-g567: 'ببینیم' is 1st-person plural optative/subjunctive ('let us see' / 'we should see'). 'تا' introduces the content/purpose clause ('so that' / 'in order to'). 'چون' here is the interrogative 'how' and 'بود' is the copula ('is/was' in classical usage) introducing the state of 'کار ما' ('our affair' — کار = affair, ما = we/our). The clause asks us to examine the state of our business/matter.
segments.s226-g568: 'چه' is an interrogative 'what' (introducing the question about manner/content). 'بازی' = 'play/game' (the action or tricking action). 'کند' is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive 'does/plays'. 'بخت' = 'fortune/fate' and 'بیدار' = 'awake/alert' modifies it ('our alert fortune'). 'ما' is the possessor 'our'. Together: 'what play does our awakened fortune make?' — i.e., what turns of fate await us.
segments.s226-g33: This is a nominal/claused predicate describing ‘they’. درستند is the third-person plural present/copula 'they are' (archaic/classical diction with the verb fused). The adjectives follow in a paratactic series: شادان دل = 'glad at heart' (شادان modifying the following noun دل), and سرفراز is another predicate adjective joined by و.
segments.s226-g34: This is a wish/negative hortative clause introduced by کز (here the fused form کزِ + ایشان = 'from them' or 'that from them') plus مبادا ('may it not be' / 'lest'), so the whole clause means 'may it not be that, from them, the world is self-sufficient.' جهان بی‌نیاز is the nominative phrase 'the world (be) without need' that مبادا negates.
segments.s226-g35: بگویم ترا is 'I tell you' (بگویم = 'I say / let me say', ترا = 'to you'); ترا‌گر is the fused conditional particle 'ترا گر' = 'if you...' and نداری is the negated present of داشتن 'you do not have' (classical ordering here). گران is an adjective 'valuable/expensive' modifying the omitted object (i.e. 'valuable things/wealth').
segments.s226-g36: گله کردن is the light-verb construction 'to complain' (گله = complaint + کردن auxiliary). کهتر از مهتران is a comparative phrase: کهتر 'the lesser/younger/lowlier', از = 'than', مهتران = 'the greater/elders/chiefs' (plural).
