The clause reads roughly 'complaining is (the act) of the lesser compared to the greater' — i.e. it is unseemly for the lesser to complain against the greater.
segments.s226-g37: بکشتم is an archaic perfective/preterital form meaning 'I planted/caused to grow' (b- prefix + کشتَم-like root), followed by the direct object درختی 'a tree' and the locative phrase بباغ اندرون 'in the inner garden' (بباغ = 'to/in the garden', اندرون = 'within/inner'). Word order is verb → object → location, a common classical poetic ordering.
segments.s226-g38: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'whose...' describing the tree. بارش = بار + ش (بار = 'load/fruit/burden', ش = possessive 'its'), so 'its load/fruit'. کبست is an obscure/archaic noun (here left untranslated as a named fruit/thing). آمد is the past 'came' (i.e. 'appeared/fell'), و links the next coordinate, برگ خون = 'leaf(s) [of] blood' (برگ = leaf, خون = blood), a striking metaphorical image.
segments.s226-g39: ز + دیده = 'from the eye(s)' (i.e. with tears). همی is a classical continuous/iterative particle meaning 'continually/was ever', inserted before the verb. آب دادم is 'I gave water' (دادم past 1st person), and برنج is the object 'rice'. The clause therefore says 'I continually gave water (with my eyes/tears) to the rice.'
segments.s226-g40: بدو = 'to it/unto it' (a dative prepositional phrase referring back to the tree/thing). بد here functions as noun 'evil/misfortune' (classical usage), مرا = 'to me' (object enclitic split in the token list as م + را), and زندگانی و گنج = 'life and treasure' are the things affected. The whole clause: 'to it came harm to me — (my) life and treasure.'
segments.s226-g113: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. کین is the noun 'vengeance/hatred' (the subject/topic), followed by سر in a metaphoric use 'head/chief' (i.e. chief concern). شهریاران is the genitive/possessor 'of the rulers/kings'. بود is the past copula 'was', so the whole clause reads literally 'When vengeance was the head of the rulers.'
segments.s226-g114: This is a verbless (nominal) clause with بود as the copula 'was' at the end. سر و کار is an idiom 'matters/affairs' (سر here part of the idiom meaning 'matter, concern'), و is the coordinating 'and', کار 'affair/matter'. با is the instrumental/prepositional 'with' (= 'by, with the means of') and تیرباران is a compound meaning 'a shower/volley of arrows'. بود again is the past copula. The sense: 'affairs were by a shower of arrows.'
segments.s226-g115: کنون is an adverb 'now'. آشتی را places 'reconciliation' as the topical/definite object (را marking it). دو راه is 'two ways' or 'two paths' and ایدرست is an archaic/adjectival predicate meaning 'proper/right' (here qualifying the two ways). The clause is verbless in surface but asserts 'now reconciliation has two proper ways.'
segments.s226-g116: نگر is an imperative 'look!/consider!'. تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that' / 'in order that' / 'to see'). شما را is the object 'you' marked with را for emphasis/definiteness. چه is the interrogative 'what', and اندرخورست is a literary/polite predicative form meaning 'is fitting' or 'suits'. So the structure is 'Consider, so that you may see what is fitting for you.'
segments.s226-g117: یکی آنک is a literary formula 'one (case) is that...' (یکی = 'one', آنک = relative 'that/which'). هر کس که is the standard relative 'whoever / whoever it is that'. از here is the partitive/agentive 'from/of', linking to خون شاه 'the king's blood' (خون = blood, شاه = king). This segment is a subordinate nominal clause whose verb appears in the next segment (typical poetic inversion).
segments.s226-g118: بگسترد is the past/optative/literary verb 'spreads/extends' (here 'spreads' or 'scatters' the blood). بر is the preposition 'upon/over', and خیره is an adjectival use meaning 'astonished/stunned' applied to this رزمگاه 'battlefield'. The object 'خون' actually precedes the verb (poetic word order): the full sense is 'whoever scatters the king's blood over this astonished battlefield.'
segments.s226-g119: ببندی and فرستی are two verbal forms in sequence functioning as imperatives/jussives: ببندی (from بست/بند) 'bind' (second-person form) and فرستی (from فرستادن) 'send'. In Persian poetry the object (him/the culprit) is omitted and understood. بر followed by شهریار marks the recipient 'to the king' (بر = 'to/toward' in this context).
segments.s226-g120: سزد‌گر is a conditional/adverbial phrase 'if it seems fitting' or 'if one deems it appropriate'. نفرماید is a literary negative/optative form of فرمودن here meaning 'will not command/should not order' (ن- prefix negates the verb in literary style). این کارزار is the object 'this battle'. Together: 'If it is deemed fitting, he will not command this battle.'
segments.s226-g353: برادرش = برادر + ش (possessive suffix) ‘his brother’. The conjunction و links the next noun فرزند ‘son’. در پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in the presence of / before’, and اوی is an archaic/poetic third‑person pronoun/correlative functioning like a locative/copula (‘he/they were there’) — the clause is an elliptical locative: ‘(they) were before him’.
segments.s226-g354: بسی here is a poetic intensifier meaning ‘many / much’. با + گهر نامور = ‘with/possessing famed jewels’; نامور modifies گهر. خویش + ش (the sequence خوی + ش) yields ‘his own’ (خویش = ‘one’s own’ formed from خوی + possessive), and the final اوی repeats the archaic locative/pronoun copula (‘there/was’) tying this phrase back to the subject — together: ‘many of his famed jewels were with him’.
segments.s226-g355: ابر دست is a compound: ابر (‘cloud’, here metaphorical/poetic) modifies دست (‘hand’ / ‘power’), i.e. ‘mighty/overarching hand’ or ‘the hand like a cloud’ as a force. The two names کیخسرو افراسیاب appear in apposition around that force; context and the following verb make it clear that کیخسرو is the agent whose hand/power affects افراسیاب.
segments.s226-g356: شود + کشته is a periphrastic passive: شود (become/happen) + past participle کشته ‘killed’ yields ‘will be killed / is to be killed’. این is the topical demonstrative ‘this’. دیده‌ام من is a perfect construction ‘I have seen’, and بخواب here means ‘in sleep’ / ‘in a dream’ (not an imperative) — so: ‘I have seen this in my sleep: (that) Afrasiab will be killed…’.
segments.s226-g357: گنهکار is the noun ‘sinner’ (here plural/general), یک تن is an emphatic ‘a single person’ and نماند is the negative of ماندن ‘to remain’ (here ‘will not remain / none will be left’). بجای (‘in place / remaining in their place’) completes the idea ‘not one sinner will remain in place’.
segments.s226-g358: مگر is the exception marker ‘except’. کشته (past participle) ‘slain’ + افگنده (‘thrown/cast’) form a compound participial idea ‘slain and cast (down)’. در زیر پای is a spatial phrase ‘under the foot(s)’ (an idiom for trampled/beneath one’s feet). Together: ‘except those slain and cast beneath the feet.’
segments.s226-g359: و لیکن = ‘and / but’ (contrastive). نخواهم is first‑person negative/future of خواستن ‘to want’ = ‘I do not want’ (or ‘I will not wish’). که introduces the subordinate clause. بر دست من is an agentive instrument/phrase ‘by my hand / at my hand’ (بر + دست + possessor من).
segments.s226-g360: شود کشته is again the passive periphrasis ‘to be slain / be made killed’. این پیر = ‘this elder / old man’ (پیر = elderly person). با انجمن ‘in/at the assembly / before the council’ specifies the occasion/context of the killing. The whole subordinate clause means ‘that this old man be killed by my hand in the assembly’, which the speaker rejects.
segments.s226-g449: ولیکن is a discourse marker meaning 'but/yet' that introduces the contrast. من is the subject 'I'. از here functions as a causal/relational preposition 'because of / on account of' and + the noun phrase خوب کردار اوی ('his good conduct') gives the reason phrase: adjective + noun + archaic possessive enclitic اوی ('his').
segments.s226-g450: نجویم is a first‑person negative present-form of جستن ('I do not seek'); in older Persian the negative prefix ن attaches directly. همی is the classical continuous/present marker placed before the verb. کین و پیکار are coordinated objects 'vengeance/hatred and battle,' and اوی here refers back to the person ('him') as the target of that enmity/battle.
segments.s226-g451: نگه کن is an imperative compound ('look! / behold!') with نگه ('look/gaze') + کن (imperative 'do'). که introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause. با شاه ایران is a prepositional phrase 'with the king of Iran' (i.e. 'the king of Iran' as the actor), and چه کرد is the interrogative/exclamatory 'what did (he) do' with کرد as the past tense verb.
segments.s226-g452: ز کار سیاوش is a topical/genitive phrase ('concerning Siavash's affair' or 'as for Siavash's case') where ز is the poetic preposition 'from/concerning'. چه is interrogative/adversative 'what/how', تیمار 'care/treatment' is the noun, and خورد is a past verb meaning 'befell/was received' (here passive/experiential sense: 'what care befell/was shown to').
segments.s226-g453: گر is the conditional 'if'. از + گفته خویش functions as 'from his spoken word / from his statement' (خویش is the reflexive/possessive 'his own' formed here as خوی + ش). باز آید is a verbal phrase 'returns' or 'retracts' (باز + آید = 'come back'), and اوی is the third‑person pronoun referring to the same person (subject 'he').
segments.s226-g454: بنزدیک ما is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'near to us' (بنزدیک = 'near/close at hand', ما = 'us'). رزم‌ساز is a nounial predicate 'war‑maker / maker of war' and آید اوی is the verb + pronoun 'he will come' — together the clause means he will approach and act as a bringer/engine of battle on our side.
segments.s226-g455: بفتراک is an adverbial 'in separation / apart' describing disposition, بر بسته ('bound/tied on') modifies کمند 'snare/lasso'. دارم is the present‑tense 'I have / I hold' and کمند is the object 'snare'. The phrase describes possession of a snare that is tied up and ready.
segments.s226-g456: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. ژنده is 'rag / ragged' and modifies پیل 'elephant' (here likely figurative 'huge one' or 'an enormous captive'); اندرآرم is first‑person singular 'I put/bring in' (اندر + آرم = 'I bring into/insert'), and ببند is an imperative 'close / bind' addressed to another participant. Together: 'Where shall I bring the ragged elephant in? Bind it!'
segments.s226-g297: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پیران is the plural noun 'elders' (subject). بیامد is the simple past 'came'. بپرده is the prepositional/locative b- + پرده 'at/behind the curtain'. سرای here is the verbal/stem sense 'to chant/recite' (classical usage), so the phrase says they began chanting at/behind the curtain.
segments.s226-g298: برفتند is past plural 'they departed/rose'. پرمایه is an attributive adjective meaning 'well‑provisioned/substantial' describing ترکان 'the Turks' (ethnonym, plural). ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from (their) place', with ز as poetic 'from' and جای 'place'.
segments.s226-g299: چو is again the temporal 'when'. A simple nominative list of proper names: هومان, نستیهن, بارمان joined by و 'and' (standard coordinate conjunction here).
segments.s226-g300: که introduces a relative/clarifying clause 'who/that'. با تیغ بودند is literally 'were with sword(s)' (با = 'with', تیغ = 'sword', بودند = past plural 'they were'); the attached گر here (in the printed word بودند‌گر) functions classically to introduce the alternative/conditional 'or/if' linking to با سنان — so the whole line means 'whether they were with sword or with spear'. سنان is the weapon 'spear' here.
segments.s226-g301: بپرسید is the classical narrative past 'he asked' (the b‑ prefix appears in older/poetic past forms). هومان is the subject (proper name). ز here is the poetic/prepositional 'about/from' and پیران سخن is the object phrase 'the elders' talk' ('سخن' = speech/topic).
segments.s226-g302: که introduces an indirect question 'what/that'. گفتارشان is 'their speech/words' (گفتار + possessive -شان). بر چه آمد is an idiomatic classical sequence meaning 'what befell/what happened' (بر = 'upon', چه = 'what', آمد = 'happened' here). به بن literally 'to the root' (بن = root/foundation) meaning 'to that fundamental point'.
segments.s226-g303: همی is an archaic/adverbial marker used in epic to indicate ongoing or customary action ('would/continually'); آشتی را places 'reconciliation' as a definite object with را; کند is the verb 'makes/does' (present/habitual/narrative sense); پایگاه is the object complement 'base/foundation' — object-first word order (آشتی را کند پایگاه = 'he makes reconciliation the base').
segments.s226-g304: وگر is the conditional/contrastive 'but if'. کینه جوید is 'hatred seeks/attempts' where جوید is a present/epic-form verb 'seeks' (third singular). سپاه از سپاه is a terse, idiomatic pairing 'army from army' meaning 'army against army' with از marking opposition in this poetic formula.
segments.s226-g1: همی رفت is a classical/perhaps poetic progressive/past action: همی adds continual or narrative aspect ('kept on / went on'). پیران is the subject (here the name Pīrān or 'the elder[s]') placed after the verb (inverted word order common in poetry). پر از + noun is the construction 'full of ...' and بیم is joined by و 'and' to list the qualities ('full of pain and fear').
segments.s226-g2: شد is the simple past/passive-like 'became' here introducing the result state. از کار رستم literally 'from/of Rostam's deed' indicates cause ('because of Rostam's action'). دلش is the possessed noun 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ـش = 'his'). به دو نیم is a result phrase 'into two halves' (به = into/towards + دو نیم = two halves).
segments.s226-g3: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came'. بنزدیک is an adverb/prepositional phrase 'near/close at hand'. ایران سپاه is the noun phrase 'the army/host of Iran' (modifier after noun is a common poetic order: 'benazdik [came] Iran sepah' = 'an Iranian host drew near').
segments.s226-g4: خروشید is past 'they roared/cried out' (an intransitive perfective). کای is the vocative 'O' addressing someone. مهتر is the addressee 'chief/headman.' رزم خواه is a compound where خواه (literally 'wants/seeks') follows رزم 'battle' to form 'battle-seeker' (i.e. 'who desires battle'); here it functions as an epithet applied to the vocative.
segments.s226-g5: شنیدم is the 1st-person past 'I heard'. کزین = کِ + زِ + این ('from this' / 'of this') introduces source. لشکر بی‌شمار is the noun phrase 'a countless army' (لشکر = host/army; بی‌شمار = innumerable).
segments.s226-g6: مرا یاد کردی is 'you remembered/mentioned me' where مرا = me (م + را; object clitic + object marker) and یاد کردن is the light-verb construction 'to remember/mention.' بهنگام کار = 'at the time of action' or 'in the midst/at the moment of the affair' (بهنگام = at the time of; کار = action/affair).
segments.s226-g7: خرامیدم is the 1st-person past 'I strode/pranced/proudly walked' (a poetic verb for strutting). از پیش آن انجمن literally 'from/in front of that assembly' (از پیش = in the presence of / from before; آن انجمن = that assembly), so the clause says the speaker approached or displayed himself before the assembly.
segments.s226-g8: بدین انجمن is 'to/into this assembly' (بدین = به + این, 'to this'); تا چه خواهی ز من is a formulaic question 'what do you want of/from me?' تا here is an interrogative particle often used in older/poetic Persian to mark 'how/what' in such formulas; چه خواهی = 'what do you want' (خواهی = 2nd-person present/future 'you desire'); ز من = 'from me'.
segments.s226-g129: بدل ('in return') here functions as an adverbial/response marker meaning 'in reply'. گفت is the past-tense verbal root 'said' (rendered here with a noun-like sense in the lexicon); پیران is the subject 'the elders'. که introduces the reported content 'that'; ژرفست is a fused adjective+copula ('is deep') modifying کار 'the affair/matter'. Word order follows classical Persian: reporting phrase + که + clause.
segments.s226-g130: ز + توران + شدن forms an infinitival/nominal phrase 'to go/turn to Turan' (ز here is the poetic/archaic prepositional marker). پیش آن شهریار means 'before/in the presence of that king' (پیش = 'in front of; before', آن = 'that', شهریار = 'monarch'). The whole segment states the specific danger/act being described.
segments.s226-g131: دگر is an adversative/adverb 'on the other hand; again'. چون is the subordinating conjunction 'when/as'. گنه‌کار is the noun 'the sinner' (subject/topic). جوید is the classical present/imperative-stem form of 'to seek/search' (here third-person present), and همی is the archaic/poetic adverb indicating continuous or habitual action (often placed with the verb).
segments.s226-g132: دل is 'the heart' (grammatically the patient/topic). از marks source/relationship 'from/of'. بیگناهان is 'the innocent' (genitive/oblique). بشوید is a present/passive-form meaning 'is washed' (classical verbal form with بـ- + present stem), and همی repeats the continuous/habitual nuance. The clause literally reads 'the heart is washed from the innocent' with a passive sense.
segments.s226-g133: بزرگان و خویشان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the great ones and the kinsmen' (بزرگان = nobles/great men; خویشان = relatives). افراسیاب follows as the name in apposition/possessive relation ('of Afrasiab'). The simple coordination is linked to the following relative clause by که.
segments.s226-g134: که is the relative pronoun 'who/which'. با introduces accompaniment/possession 'with/possessing'. گنج 'treasure' and تختند ('they are on thrones' / 'they have thrones') form the first pair; then و با جاه و آب parallels that construction ('and with rank and water'), where آب here is figurative for 'wealth' or 'substance'. تختند is a third-person plural predicate ('are enthroned' / 'sit on thrones').
segments.s226-g135: ازین در is a fused locative phrase 'from this door/point' (از = 'from', ین = orthographic variant of این 'this', در = 'door/point'). کجا is the interrogative 'where'. گفت is the past tense 'said/spoke'. یارم is 'my friend/companion' (with enclitic -م), and سخن is 'word/speech'. The clause asks 'From this place/point, where did my companion speak?'
segments.s226-g136: نه ... نه is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor'. سر 'head' and بن 'root/foundation' are the two items negated. باشد is the present/subjunctive copular verb 'be' (here marking existence/possession in classical style). این آرزو را places 'this desire' as the topic/object (را marks definiteness). The whole means 'This desire should have neither head nor root' or more simply 'This wish has neither head nor foundation.'
segments.s226-g281: شما (you) is the addressed subject (plural/formal) placed before the verb. یکسره modifies چشم and means "completely/entirely." چشم بر من is the object phrase (چشم + preposition بر + object من = "eyes upon me"). نهید is the verb (2nd-person plural form of نهادن here: "place/keep"); the order is Subject — adverb — object — verb, typical of classical Persian prose/poetry.
segments.s226-g282: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning "when." من is the 1st-person subject. برخروشم is a 1st-person verbal form built on خروش with the prefix بر- meaning "my uproar/anger rises." دمید is a past (or perfective) verb "blew/issued forth" describing the event; و links to the next verb. دهید is 2nd-person (plural) present/subjunctive/imperative of دادن here with the sense "cry/shout." The clause reads: "when I become wrathful and (that) blows forth, you cry out."
segments.s226-g283: همانا is a discourse particle meaning "verily/truly" introducing an emphatic statement. که here is the complementizer/connector introducing the clause about جنگ‌آوران ("warriors"). جنگ‌آوران is the topic (plural noun) that will be characterized by the following clause.
segments.s226-g284: فزون precedes باشد to form a comparative idea "be more/exceeding." باشد is the verbal copula (classical subjunctive/optative form used in reported or emphatic statements, here rendered as simple "are/number"). از ما indicates the comparison "than us." دلیر و سوار are predicate adjectives/nouns describing those warriors ("brave and mounted/riders"). The literal order is "more be than us brave and mounted."
segments.s226-g285: ز is the poetic preposition "from/because of." یک تن is an indefinite singular "one person/man." چنین is an adverb "thus/so." زار and پیچان are predicative adjectives describing the state resulting from that one person: زار "wretched/miserable," پیچان "twisting/turned about" (a poetic word indicating disarray or being thrown into confusion). شدیم is the 1st-person plural past/resultant verb "we became/we were made." The line gives cause (from one man) followed by the resulting state.
segments.s226-g286: همه "all/everyone" is the subject; پاک here functions adverbially/intensifying as "utterly/entirely." ناکشته is an older/ambiguous form (see note below). بیجان is a predicate adjective "lifeless/dead." شدیم repeats the 1st-person plural result "we became/were left." Together the phrase states that all of them ended up entirely lifeless.
segments.s226-g287: چنان دان is an imperative/hortative construction: دان (from دانستن/دانیدن used in the sense "consider/know") with چنان "thus" = "consider it so/so know." که introduces the following descriptive clause. او is the 3rd-person pronoun "he." ژنده modifies the following noun as "ragged/mean-looking." پیلست is an uncommon/archaic word here (treated as a noun referring to an elephant-man/elephant-driver or similar role). مست is an adjective "drunk/intoxicated" applied to that person.
segments.s226-g288: به آوردگه is a prepositional phrase meaning "at/to the arena/place of performance" (به + آوردگه). شیر is the direct object "lion." گیرد is the verb (3rd-person singular present/optative of گرفتن, "seizes/takes"). بدست is an adverbial phrase "by the hand/in hand" placed after the verb to indicate how he seizes the lion ("seizes the lion by the hand").
segments.s226-g361: که introduces a relative/clausal modifier here ('who/for that'). او را is an older dative/topic construction meaning 'for him' (را marks the affected person, not a direct object here). به جز ... نیست is an idiom: به جز + noun = 'except'; پیشه 'profession/practice' follows راستی 'honesty/uprightness' to form the compound 'راستی پیشه' = 'one whose practice is honesty'. نیست is the existential negation 'is not / does not have'.
segments.s226-g362: ز بد 'from/away from evil' gives the source; بر دلش 'upon his heart' is a locative phrase (بر + دل + enclitic ش = 'on his heart'). راه اندیشه is a compound 'path of thought' = 'a way for thought'; نیست negates the whole: 'there is no way for evil thought into his heart.' Word order is poetic but follows classical Persian syntax: source — locative — predicate.
segments.s226-g363: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic adverbial (here 'now/perhaps/perchance'). باز آرد is the verb phrase 'brings back / repeats' (آرد = 'brings' in classical Persian); این را که گفت is the object clause 'this which he said' (که introduces the relative). The whole protasis means 'if he now/again brings up this that he said.'
segments.s226-g364: گناه گذشته is a noun phrase 'past sin(s)'. بباید is the impersonal/modal 'one must / it ought to' and is followed by نهفت, an older poetic form meaning 'be hidden' or 'conceal' (here understood as passive/obligatory concealment). The clause expresses obligation: 'past sins must be concealed.'
segments.s226-g365: گنهکار is the noun 'the sinner' (agent). با خواسته هرچ بود is a prepositional+relative phrase 'with whatever desire he had' (با = 'with'; خواسته = 'wish/desire'; هرچ = 'whatever'; بود is the past of 'to be'). The whole phrase modifies the action that follows.
segments.s226-g366: سپارد (classical form of سپرد) = 'he entrusted / left' and takes بما ('to us', dative). کین 'grudge/vengeance' is the thing entrusted. نباید + فزود is a prohibition/impersonal modal 'it must not increase' (نباید = 'must not'; فزود = 'increase'); the final clause comments on the entrusted grudge: it ought not be allowed to grow.
segments.s226-g367: ازین پس ('from this/after this') is a temporal phrase meaning 'from now on'. مرا is actually split here as م + را in the manuscript: it functions as the dative/experiencer 'for me'. جای پیکار نیست is an existential negation 'there is no place for battle' (جای = place; پیکار = battle; نیست = 'is not / there is none'). The construction puts the affected person (مرا) before the predicate, typical of classical word order.
segments.s226-g368: به از is an idiomatic comparative 'better than / preferable to'. راستی 'honesty/uprightness' is the standard of comparison. در جهان کار نیست is another existential negation 'there is no deed/work in the world' (در = 'in'; جهان = 'the world'; کار = 'deed/work'); overall: 'There is no deed in the world better than honesty.'
segments.s226-g97: A poetic enumerative noun phrase naming peoples/peoples' names joined by the coordinating conjunction و. Each item is an ethnonym/place-name; و is the simple connective 'and'. There is no verb here — this is a nominal list that will be located by the following prepositional phrase.
segments.s226-g98: A prepositional phrase giving the geographical span: ازین = از + این ('from this'), مرز 'border/limit', تا 'as far as', پیش 'before/in front of', دریای سند 'the Sea of Sind' (دریای is the ezāfe/construct 'sea of ...'). Word order is typical Persian: source (ازین مرز) then endpoint (تا پیش دریای سند).
segments.s226-g99: A prepositional/causal phrase: ز (poetic from/because of) + خون سیاوش 'Siavash's blood' (genitive), followed by همه بیگناه 'all the innocent' as the affected group/subject. The phrase sets the cause/reason (why the army was mustered).
segments.s226-g100: سپاهی is a collective noun meaning 'an army/force' or 'soldiers'; کشیده is the past participle of کشیدن used here in a verbal phrase 'have drawn/brought' (perfective sense), and بدین is the archaic/contractional form of به+این meaning 'to this'; رزمگاه is the place 'battlefield'. The structure reads '[they] have brought an army to this battlefield' with the agent implied in the preceding phrase.
segments.s226-g101: ترا is the dative/enclitic 'for you' (here split as ت + را in the segmentation), آشتی 'reconciliation' is the noun, بهتر 'better' is a comparative predicate, and آید 'comes' is an impersonal/predictive verb 'it is better.' که begins the explanatory clause; here it functions as 'for/because' introducing the reason (why reconciliation is better). جنگ stands at the start of that reason clause ('war...').
segments.s226-g102: نباید is the impersonal modal 'one should not / it must not'; گرفتن is the verbal infinitive 'to take/undertake' (here 'to undertake' or 'to take up' — i.e. 'to wage/enter into [war]'), چنین کار 'such a deed/affair', and تنگ literally 'narrow' but idiomatically 'ill-advised/difficult/dangerous' in this context. The clause prohibits undertaking such an affair.
segments.s226-g103: نگر is the imperative 'look/consider'. تا introduces the purposive/subordinating clause 'to/so that', چه 'what', بینی 'you see' (2nd-person present/subjunctive or simple present 'you see/will see'), تو 'you' and داناتری is a comparative 'more knowledgeable/wiser' — the clause invites the addressee to look and note that he is wiser.
segments.s226-g104: برزم is a locative/adverbial expression 'in battle' (archaic/poetic formation meaning 'on the field of battle'), دلیران 'the brave (ones)' and تواناتری is a comparative 'more powerful/more able'. The phrase parallels the previous comparative (داناتری) to say that, in battle, he is braver and better able.
segments.s226-g41: مرا زو همه رنج بهر آمدست is arranged with the experiencer first (مرا = to me / me with the object clitic را), then the source (زو = from him), the noun phrase (همه رنج = all suffering), and the predicate (بهر آمدست). بهر آمدست is an older/poetic perfective/passive-like expression meaning 'has come upon / has befallen'; بهر here combines with آمدست to indicate what has happened to the speaker.
segments.s226-g42: کزو بار تریاک زهر آمدست begins with the relative/source кَ- + ز (کزو = 'from whom / from which'), then بار 'a load/burden', followed by a noun‑compound تریاک زهر 'opium-poison' (object/thing that has come), and the same archaic perfect آمدست 'has come'. The phrase literally says 'from whom a burden of opium-poison has come.'
segments.s226-g43: سیاوش مرا چون پدر داشتی is a vocative + statement in the 2nd person past. سیاوش is the addressee; مرا (م + را) places 'me' as object/emphasized patient; چون پدر is a simile 'as a father' modifying the relation; داشتی is the 2nd‑person singular past of داشتن 'you had/treated'.
segments.s226-g44: به پیش بدیها سپر داشتی has the prepositional phrase به پیش بدیها 'before/in the face of evils' (به + پیش acting as 'in front of / against' + plural بدیها), then the noun سپر 'shield' and the same past verb داشتی 'you had/bore'. The clause means 'you bore/kept a shield against evils.'
segments.s226-g45: بسا درد و سختی و رنجا که من lists goods: بسا 'many' quantifies the nouns درد 'pain', سختی 'hardship', and رنجا (here 'afflictions' / afflicted things). که introduces a relative clause that follows and refers back to these experiences; من is the subject of the following verb.
segments.s226-g46: کشیدم ازان شاه و زان انجمن is the relative clause with کشیدم (1st‑person past) from the verb کشیدن used in the sense 'to endure / to suffer' here. ازان and زان are poetic forms of 'از آن' meaning 'from that', and they take the agents شاه 'the king' and انجمن 'the assembly' respectively: 'I endured (them) from that king and that assembly.'
segments.s226-g47: گوای من اندر جهان ایزدست has the nominal phrase گوای من 'my witness' (گوای = witness), the locative/scene marker اندر جهان 'in the world', and the predicate ایزدست = ایزد + ست (poetic 'it is God / God is'). Read as 'God is my witness in the world.'
segments.s226-g48: گوا خواستن دادگر را بدست is an elliptic clause: گوا خواستن 'to call as witness / to seek as witness' (گوا = witness, خواستن = infinitive 'to ask/call'), followed by دادگر را 'the just one' marked with را as object, and بدست 'by/into the hand' meaning 'is in (the) hand/authority'. Together with the previous clause it yields 'to call the just as witness is in His hand' (i.e. God's authority).
segments.s226-g553: پس is a discourse connector meaning 'then'. The following nouns پشت و دست چپ و دست راست are a coordinated list of body-parts ('his back and (his) left hand and (his) right hand'); Persian often omits the possessive pronoun, so these are understood as belonging to the actor referenced earlier. The sequence is an adverbial/nominal phrase that will be the object of the verb in the next segment.
segments.s226-g554: بجنگ اندر آورده is a verbal phrase: بجنگ ('into the fight') + اندر آورده (past form of آوردن used here as a causative/putting-into action, literally 'brought/put into'). زان سو که خواست is an adverbial relative: زان سو ('that side') + که introduces the relative clause whose verb is خواست (past 'he wished/desired'), so the whole tail means 'toward the side he wished.'
segments.s226-g555: چو ... دید is a temporal subordinating clause: چو 'when' + پیران 'the elders' (subject) + چنان 'thus/that' (object/degree) + دید 'saw' (past). دل شاد کرد is the following main clause: دل 'heart' (object, with possessor understood) + شاد 'glad' + کرد 'made' (past), i.e. 'they made their hearts glad' = 'they rejoiced.'
segments.s226-g556: ز رزم تهمتن introduces cause/source: ز 'from/because of' + رزم تهمتن 'the battle/fighting of Tahmatan' (رزم + the name/epithet تهمتن). دل آزاد کرد is a verbal clause: دل '(their) heart' + آزاد 'free' + کرد 'made' (past) — 'the fighting of Tahmatan made their hearts free' (i.e. relieved them).
segments.s226-g557: بهومان چنین گفت is a clause meaning 'he said to us thus': بهومان is an indirect-object phrase ('to us') here (note: the tokenization/senses supplied do not match this use), چنین 'thus', گفت is the past verb 'said'. کامروز کار is the beginning of the quoted content or object ('today's deed/work'). The verb گفت is past and introduces the wish that follows.
segments.s226-g558: بکام دل ما کند روزگار is a subjunctive/optative wish-clause whose verb is کند (present subjunctive 'may/might do'). بکام 'to the liking/pleasure of' + دل ما 'our heart(s)' is the object/goal, and روزگار 'the course of time/fate' is the grammatical subject that 'may make' (کند) something happen. The overall sense: 'May fate make (today's) deed turn out to the desire of our hearts.'
segments.s226-g559: بدین ساز is an adverbial phrase 'with this tune/in this way' (بدین = 'with this' in older/poetic Persian; tokenization here separates the prefix), followed by و چندین سوار دلیر 'and several brave riders/horsemen' — a coordinated adverbial/nominal phrase describing how or with whom the action is done. The demonstrative/old-fashioned form بدین is typical of epic/poetic register.
segments.s226-g560: سرافراز هر یک بکردار شیر is a predicative nominal phrase describing those riders: سرافراز 'proud/honored' modifies the group; هر یک 'each one' + بکردار 'of good conduct/virtuous' + شیر 'lion' (simile/metaphor). The copula 'was/are' is omitted (a normal omission in Persian), so the phrase reads 'each one (was) a proud, virtuous lion.'
segments.s226-g513: مرا گفته بودی: the sequence places the object 'مرا' (to me) before the compound verb 'گفته بودی' (past participle + past of بودن), a past‑perfect/pluperfect construction meaning 'you had told (me)'. The particle 'که' introduces the reported clause. 'فردا پگاه' is a temporal adverbial 'tomorrow at dawn' placed at the start of the subordinate clause.
segments.s226-g514: ز هر سو (prepositional phrase) = 'from every side' (ز + هر سو). بجنگ is the infinitive/subjunctival form 'to fight' (the b‑prefix marks the non‑past/subjunctive/infinitive in classical Persian). اندر آرم سپاه is a verb complex with اندر (into/within) + آرم (first person verb form here used poetically to mean 'I put/bring/place') and سپاه as its object 'the army' — together: 'I will bring the army into battle.'
segments.s226-g515: وزان پس = 'and/then after that' — classical connective + temporal adverb. ز رستم is the prepositional object 'from/with Rostam' (= 'against Rostam'), and بجویم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive 'I seek' (ب + جویم). نبرد is the noun object 'battle' — 'I will seek battle with Rostam.'
segments.s226-g516: سرش را is the object 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش = 'his', plus را as the definite object marker). ز ابر gives a locative/ornamental phrase 'on/from a cloud'. اندرآرم is a first‑person verb 'I place/put into' (classical compound), and بگرد (here) functions as a jussive/subjunctive/imperative‑like verb 'make it turn' or 'it shall turn' — together: 'I will place his head on a cloud and make it revolve/turn.'
segments.s226-g517: بدو گفت شنگل = 'to him Shangal said' — بدو is the dative 'to him', گفت is the reporting verb 'said' and شنگل is the speaker's name; word order (dative + verb + subject) is typical of classical narration. من از گفت خویش begins the quoted speech: 'I, from my saying/word…' (خویش = 'my own').
segments.s226-g518: نگردم is negated present/subjunctive 'I will not turn/change/abandon' (ن‑ + گردم). نبینی here functions in the clause that follows as 'you shall see' (classical narrative uses present/subjunctive forms for future/result), and ز من کم و بیش = 'from me, less or more' (an idiomatic way of saying 'you will see some measure/amount from me' — i.e. you will see what I do).
segments.s226-g519: هم‌اکنون = 'right now/at once'. شوم is a first‑person subjunctive/hortative 'I will go / I shall be' used here to indicate immediate action. پیش این گرد = 'in front of/before this [band/host]' (پیش = in the presence of), and گیر is a deverbal/imperative‑flavored word 'seize/take' used poetically to indicate the act of grabbing or attacking the host.
segments.s226-g520: تنش را = 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش = 'his' + را object marker). کنم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of کردن 'I will make/do', here causatively introducing the resulting state. پاره‌پاره 'in pieces' describes the manner, and بتیر ('با تیر' contracted) means 'by arrows' — together: 'I will have his body made into pieces by arrows.'
segments.s226-g377: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning "when." بشنید is a perfective past (classical form of شنیدن) meaning "he heard." The rest of the clause, گودرز بر پای خاست, is a simple past narrative: گودرز (subject) + بر پای خاست (an idiomatic phrase, literally "rose onto his feet" = "stood up"); خاست is the archaic past of خاستن.
segments.s226-g378: بدو گفت = به + او + گفت, where بدو is the dative clitic phrase "to him." گفت here is the past reporting verb 'said' (the provided sense list didn't include this verbal sense, so -1 is used). کای is a poetic vocative "O!", followed by the addressed titles/epithets مهتر راد و راست ("chief / noble and upright"); و links the two adjectives.
segments.s226-g379: ستون سپاهی is a compound-like expression: ستون ("pillar/support") qualified by سپاهی (military/of the army) — together meaning "the army's pillar" or mainstay. و simply links this phrase to the following epithet (زیبای گاه), where زیبای is an adjective meaning "beautiful, ornament of" and گاه (here) functions as "place/court/time" (classical usage), heading the phrase.
segments.s226-g380: فروزان is an adjectival/predicative adjective meaning "radiant/bright." بتو is the dative pronoun "to you," which places the blessing or attribute on the addressee. The following coordination شاه و تخت و کلاه lists the royal items (king, throne, crown/helmet) that are being wished or described as luminous toward the addressee; the copula ('is' / 'may') is implied in poetic style.
segments.s226-g381: سر مایه تست is a classical construction: سر مایه = "chief source / principal wealth," and تست is the archaic/cliticized copular/genitive element meaning "is yours" (classical contraction of تو است). روشن modifies خرد, giving "enlightened/clear wisdom." Overall this is a predicative clause: 'Your chief possession is bright wisdom.'
segments.s226-g382: روانت is روان + enclitic ت "your spirit/soul." همی is the older modal particle indicating continual/habitual or optative force ("may it always..."). از خرد بر خورد uses the verbal sense of خوردن in a periphrastic compound بر خوردن meaning "to meet/encounter/befall" — here meaning "your soul may always encounter or be endowed with wisdom." The construction is optative/blessing in tone.
segments.s226-g383: ز جنگ آشتی is a comparative or evaluative phrase where ز (poetic from/of) sets up the contrast: 'from war, reconciliation...' بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly' and بهترست is an archaic copular/predicative form meaning 'is better.' Together: 'Peace (reconciliation) is surely better than war.'
segments.s226-g384: نگه کن is an imperative idiom meaning 'take heed' / 'be careful' (نگه here functions as 'watch/heed' and کن is the imperative 'do'). که introduces the content clause. گاوت is گاو + enclitic -ت 'your cow.' بچرم is first-person present/optative 'I graze / I may graze' (classical verbal form used to express action that is happening or may happen); اندرست is 'is within/inside.' Syntactically the clause is a warning: 'beware, for your cow is grazing inside/among [mine].'
segments.s226-g321: تن خویش is a possessive phrase: تن (body/person) + the enclitic ش (here forming خویش = 'one's own/yourself'), so "yourself." یکباره is an adverb (“at once/suddenly”) modifying غمگین (“sad”). مکن is the classical negative imperative (مکن = «do not»), so the clause literally commands “do not make your body/soul suddenly sad.”
segments.s226-g322: مگر here introduces an exception meaning “unless.” کز is the old contracted form of «که از/که» (i.e. “that/from which”) and begins the subordinate clause. گمان دیگر = “some other thought/suspicion” (گمان = suspicion), and اید is an optative/subjunctive sense “may come/should come” (impersonal here: “words may arise”). سخن is the subject of اید (“words/speech”). The whole clause means “unless words come from another suspicion.”
segments.s226-g323: بنا آمده is a classical idiom: بنا (establishment/foundation) + آمده (has come) yields “has come to be / has become established.” کار دل را = “the affair/work of the heart,” with را marking the definite object. بغم is a prepositional phrase “in/with sorrow” placed after the object. Together: “sorrow has come to be the heart's concern.”
segments.s226-g324: سزدگر is a conditional/optative compound (سزد + گر) meaning “if it befit / it may be fitting.” نداری here denotes lack/absence (i.e. “if you have no reason / if you lack [a cause]”); the phrase is elliptical. نباشی is a negative present/imperative form “do not be” (classical 2nd‑person negative), and دژم is the predicate adjective “sullen/grim.” Thus: “If it be fitting (that sorrow touch the heart), — if you have no cause, do not be sullen.”
segments.s226-g325: وزین روی is an attributive phrase: وزین (weighty, dignified) qualifying روی (face/countenance) — “with that grave/dignified face.” The clause Rustom یلان را بخواند uses را as the definite object marker on یلان (the heroes) and بخواند is the third‑person past/epic form “he called/summoned.” Word order places the descriptive phrase before the proper name and the verb at the end, as typical in classical Persian verse.
segments.s226-g326: سخنهای is the plural (or genitive) of سخن “words/speech,” followed by the postpositive adjective بایسته “fitting/proper.” چندی is a small-quantity/temporary adverb “a few / for a while,” which in this idiom precedes the verb. براند is an archaic 3rd‑person verb form (from راندن) used here in a formulaic sense “he put forth/uttered” (in classical verse راندن can collocate with سخن to mean ‘to say a few words’).
segments.s226-g327: چو is a particle meaning “as/like” (here introducing examples) and is repeated before proper names as a poetic listing device. The proper names follow in coordination with و (“and”). The sequence is simply a roster of heroes; there is no new verb in this segment.
segments.s226-g328: This continues the list of names joined by و. In epic lists like this the final names may be written as two words (here خراد نیو) even though they form a single proper name; the grammar is simply coordinate enumeration.
segments.s226-g9: بدو is the allative clitic ‘to him/unto him’ placed before the speech verb; گفت is the past tense ‘said’ (the exact sense 'said' is not present in the supplied senses list); رستم is the speaker. که introduces the content of the reported speech, and نام تو چیست is an embedded question with the interrogative چی before the copula ست (archaic literary order: interrogative + copula).
segments.s226-g10: بدین آمدن is a prepositional / nominalized phrase meaning ‘in/for this coming’ (بدین = ‘to/for this’ + آمدن ‘coming’). رای و کام تو is a coordinated noun phrase ‘your counsel and your desire’; چی is the interrogative and ست is the copula, yielding the question ‘what is your plan and desire (in coming here)?’ Note the literary order with the copula at the end.
segments.s226-g11: چنین داد پاسخ is a compact past phrasing ‘thus gave answer’ (چنین = thus; داد past of دادن ‘gave’; پاسخ = answer). که introduces the content of that answer. پیران منم is a predicative nominal with the emphatic copula منم ‘I am’—here likely giving the name/title 'Pīrān' or identifying himself as one of the elders.
segments.s226-g12: سپهدار is a title/office ‘commander’ and is followed by the definite demonstrative phrase این شیر گیران ‘these lion‑seizers’ (شیر = lion; گیران = ‘seizers/catchers’ used as a noun). The final منم ‘I am’ repeats the identification: ‘I am the commander of these…’. Word order is title + possessed phrase + copula (literary).
segments.s226-g13: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/of’; هومان and ویسه are proper names (Homan, Viseh). مرا (here split in the annotation as م + را) is the first‑person object ‘me’, and خواستی is second‑person past of خواستن ‘you asked/wanted’. The natural reading is ‘you asked me for Viseh of Homan’ or ‘you sought Viseh, son of Homan, from me.’
segments.s226-g14: بخوبی is an adverb ‘well’; زبان را uses the definite object marker را with زبان ‘tongue/speech’ (here meaning ‘your words/speech’). بیاراستی is an archaic/past form meaning ‘you arrayed/adjusted/phrased’ — ‘you put your words well’ — with the verb in the second‑person past. The clause praises the way the interlocutor formulated his speech.
segments.s226-g15: دلم تیز شد is an idiomatic past construction ‘my heart became sharp/keen’ meaning ‘my heart leaped/I became eager’. تا here introduces the ensuing subordinate clause with a purposive/result sense (‘so that’ / ‘in order that’), and تو از مهتران places the addressee among or relative to the مهتران ‘chiefs/leaders’ (annotation’s lemma for مهتران is not ideal but the function is ‘chiefs’).
segments.s226-g16: کدامی is the interrogative predicate ‘which one / which of you are (you)?’ followed by the partitive/prepositional ز ‘of’. گردان is a collective ‘troop/host’ and جنگ آوران is a compound meaning ‘war‑bringers’ i.e. warriors; together they form ‘which of the host of warriors are you?’ — a direct question about the addressee’s rank or place among fighters.
segments.s226-g81: بمن بر کنون جای بخشایشست = lit. 'to/for me upon-now place forgiveness-is.' The prepositional phrase بمن (to me/for me) comes first (dative/benefactive). بر کنون functions adverbially ('at present'); جای is the noun 'place/room'; بخشایشست is بخشایش (pardon/forgiveness) + the poetic fused copula -ست (old form of است) meaning 'is'. The clause therefore states existence/availability of pardon for the speaker at the present time.
segments.s226-g82: سپاه اندر آوردن آرایشست = 'to bring/assemble the army is an array/show.' سپاه اندر آوردن is a noun phrase built with the infinitive آوردن ('to bring, to muster') governed by سپاه ('army'); آرایشست is آرایش ('array, ornament, arrangement') + fused copula -ست. The infinitival phrase is the logical subject and آرایشست predicates that this muster is essentially an array or display.
segments.s226-g83: اگر نیستی بر دلم درد و غم = conditional 'if there is not pain and sorrow on my heart.' اگر introduces the condition. نیستی is an archaic/poetic negative existential (نیست) here meaning 'there is not.' بر دلم ('upon my heart') places the emotion; note دلم is دل + possessive -م ('my heart'). درد و غم are the two coordinated nouns 'pain and sorrow' that are said not to exist in that place.
segments.s226-g84: ازین تخمه جز کشتن پیلسم = 'from this seed/lineage, except killing Pilasm.' ازین (از + این) = 'from this, of this'; تخمه here means 'offspring/lineage.' جز introduces the exception ('except'); کشتن is the infinitive 'killing' and پیلسم is the name/object. The phrase says that, of this stock, nothing will happen/exists except that Pilasm be killed.
segments.s226-g85: جز او نیز چندی دلیر و جوان = 'apart from him, also a few brave and young (ones).' جز او means 'besides/except him'; نیز adds 'also.' چندی is an indefinite quantifier 'several/a few'; دلیر و جوان are coordinated adjectives 'brave and young' modifying an implicit noun (e.g. مردان) that is omitted, a common classical usage.
segments.s226-g86: که در جنگ سیر آمدند از روان = relative clause 'who in the battle came ... from spirit.' که introduces the relative clause ('who/which'); در جنگ = 'in battle.' سیر آمدند is past plural 'they came' with سیر modifying the coming (here idiomatically meaning something like 'in force/with full heart' or 'with a spirited advance' in classical usage); آمدند is the past plural verb. از روان literally 'from the spirit' — idiomatically 'with spirit' or 'with heart/enthusiasm.'
segments.s226-g87: ازین پس مرا بیم جانست نیز = 'from now on (ازین پس) to me there is fear for life as well.' ازین پس is the temporal phrase 'from this time on/henceforth.' مرا is the object/benefactive pronoun 'to me'; بیم جانست is a nominal predicate meaning roughly 'fear (for) life exists' (بیم = fear; جانست = 'is life' or 'is dear' in the poetic copula form), so the whole means the speaker is fearful for his life. نیز 'also' adds that this is an additional concern.
segments.s226-g88: سخن چند گویم ز فرزند و چیز = 'I will say a few words about (my) child/descendant and matters.' سخن چند = 'a few words' (سخن = speech/words; چند = a few/several). گویم is the first-person present/subjunctive of گفتن 'I say / I will say' used here to state the speaker's intent. ز (poetic form of از) introduces the topic 'about/from' and فرزند و چیز = 'the son/descendant and other matters.'
segments.s226-g521: This clause begins with the source prepositional pronoun ازو (az-u, 'from him'), followed by the noun کین (kin, 'vengeance'). کاموس here is an adjectival/poetic intensifier attached to کین (literally a vast/oceanic hatred). جویم is first-person singular present-subjunctive/optative ('I seek / I shall seek') and takes the following verbal complement بجنگ ('to fight' / 'in battle'); grammatically جویم + بجنگ = 'I seek (it) in battle' or 'I will seek to fight.'
segments.s226-g522: بایرانیان is the affected party ('the Iranians') and is followed by the preposition/particle بر (here 'against / upon'), then the verb کنم in first-person singular ('I will make / I will do'). کار تنگ is an idiomatic nominal phrase: کار ('affair, condition') modified by تنگ ('narrow, tight'), so کار تنگ کردن = 'to put (someone) into a tight/pressured situation.' The clause therefore reads 'I will (do/make) a tight situation upon the Iranians.'
segments.s226-g523: هم آنگه is a temporal/contrastive phrase ('then / even then'). سپه را uses the object/definite marker را after سپه ('the host/army'). بسه here means 'sufficient / enough' and بهر کرد is a light-verb construction ('to make/prepare for a purpose'); together سپه را بسه بهر کرد = 'he made the army sufficient/ready for the purpose.' Note the classical/poetic word order with the object marked by را before the verbal phrase.
segments.s226-g524: This is a compact sequence of actions: بزد کوس ('he struck the war-drum') places verb before object as usual; وز دشت برخاست گرد is a small descriptive clause where وز functions as a noun 'a gust/blast (from)' tied to دشت ('the plain') and برخاست گرد means 'round about rose' with گرد used as 'dust.' Together: 'he struck the drum; a gust/dust from the plain arose.'
segments.s226-g525: برفتند is third-person plural past tense ('they went / advanced'). یک بهره is a noun phrase 'one share / one detachment' and با introduces a companion/instrumental phrase; ژنده پیل is a compound describing that companion: ژنده ('ragged') modifying پیل ('elephant'). The phrase thus means 'they advanced — one detachment with a ragged elephant.'
segments.s226-g526: This sentence uses a stative past: سپه بود ('the host was' / 'there was a host'). صف برکشیده is a participial compound where صف ('battle-line') is modified by برکشیده ('drawn out, raised'), and the measure دو میل follows to quantify the line's extent. Poetic inversion places the copula and noun before the modifier: 'the army was, a line drawn two miles (long).' Note برکشیده is a past participle functioning as adjective.
segments.s226-g527: سر پیلبان is a genitive/possessive nominal phrase ('the head of the mahout/elephant-driver'). پر ز is a common poetic construction meaning 'full of' (پر = 'full', ز = 'of/from'), so پر ز رنگ و نگار means 'full of color and painted designs.' Note the absence of an ezāfe — typical in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s226-g528: همه پاک is a brief predicative phrase: همه ('all / everyone') + پاک (here adverbial/'neatly' or 'completely'). با introduces the instruments/ornaments (افسر 'helmet' and گوشوار 'ear-ornament'). The clause means 'all were clean/neatly (i.e., properly) with helmets and ear-ornaments' — i.e., uniformly adorned.
